Ayahs

وَٱلنَّجْمِ إِذَا هَوَىٰ (١)
By the star when it sets: (1)
مَا ضَلَّ صَاحِبُكُمْ وَمَا غَوَىٰ (٢)
your companion has neither gone astray, nor amiss. (2)
وَمَا يَنطِقُ عَنِ ٱلْهَوَىٰٓ (٣)
Nor does he speak out of [his own] desire: (3)
إِنْ هُوَ إِلَّا وَحْىٌ يُوحَىٰ (٤)
it is just a revelation that is revealed [to him], (4)

Content of Surah An-Najm

According to some, this Surah is the first Surah that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) recited openly and with a loud voice in the Sanctuary of Mecca after making his invitation public. The polytheists listened to it, and all the believers of that day, and even the polytheists, prostrated! [^1] According to some commentators, this Surah was revealed in the holy month of Ramadan in the fifth year of the Bi'thah (the start of the prophetic mission). ^2 Some have said that this Surah is the first Surah in which the verse of obligatory prostration (Sajdah) was revealed; [^3] however, considering that according to the well-known report, Surah "Iqra" was revealed before it and contains a verse of prostration at its end, this report seems unlikely.

In any case, due to its "Meccan" nature, this Surah contains discussions on the principles of belief, especially "Prophethood" and "Resurrection" (Ma'ad), and it addresses the awakening of the disbelievers with crushing threats and repeated warnings.

The content of this Surah can be summarized into seven sections:

1- At the beginning of the Surah, after a meaningful oath, it speaks of the reality of revelation (Wahy) and clarifies the direct contact of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) with the messenger of revelation, "Gabriel," and absolves the holy presence of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) from saying anything other than divine revelation.

[^1]: Tafsir Ruh al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 208.

[^3]: Tafsir al-Maraghi, Vol. 27, p. 41.

2- In another part of this Surah, it speaks of the Ascension (Mi'raj) of the Prophet and depicts aspects of it with short and meaningful phrases, which also has a direct relationship with revelation.

3- It then addresses the superstitions of the polytheists regarding idols, the worship of angels, and other matters that were based on nothing but whims and desires, severely rebuking them in this regard, warning them against worshipping them, and proving this point with powerful logic.

4- In another section, it opens the path of repentance to these deviants and all sinners, promising them the "vast forgiveness" of the Truth (Allah), and emphasizes that everyone is responsible for their own actions and no one shall bear the burden of another's sin.

5- To complete these objectives, another part of this Surah reflects aspects of the issue of "Resurrection" and provides clear evidence for this matter from what exists in the worldly life.

6- As is customary, it makes references to the painful fate of previous nations who persisted in enmity against the Truth with stubbornness and obstinacy (such as the peoples of 'Ad, Thamud, Noah, and Lot) to awaken the heedless through this means.

7- Finally, the Surah concludes with the command to prostrate and worship the Lord.

Among the distinctions of this Surah are its short verses and their specific rhythm, which gives its concepts a deep influence, awakening the hearts and souls of the sleepers and carrying them toward the heavens.

Additionally, the naming of this Surah as "An-Najm" (The Star) is due to the first verse of this Surah.

The Virtue of Reciting This Surah

In traditions, significant virtues have been mentioned for the recitation of this Surah:

In a Hadith from the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله), we read: "Whoever recites Surah An-Najm, Allah will grant him ten rewards for every person who believed in Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) and those who denied him." [^1]

And in a Hadith from Imam Sadiq, Ja'far ibn Muhammad (عليه السلام), we read: "Whoever makes a habit of reciting 'Wa-Najm' every day or every night, will live praised among the people, will be forgiven, and will gain popularity among the people." ^2

Certainly, such great rewards belong to those who make the recitation of this Surah a means for reflection and then action, and whose lives are illuminated by the various teachings of this Surah.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 9, p. 170. ^2: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 92, p. 305.

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ وَ النَّجْمِ إِذا هَوى (1) ما ضَلَّ صاحِبُكُمْ وَ ما غَوى (2) وَ ما يَنْطِقُ عَنِ الْهَوى (3) إِنْ هُوَ إِلاَّ وَحْيٌ يُوحى (4)

Commentary

It is noteworthy that the previous Surah (Surah At-Tur) ended with the word "An-Nujum" (the stars), and this Surah begins with the word "An-Najm" (the star) by which Allah has sworn, saying: "By the star when it goes down" (وَ النَّجْمِ إِذا هَوى).

Regarding what is meant by "An-Najm" here, commentators have offered many possibilities, each choosing an interpretation.

A group considers it a reference to the "Glorious Quran," as it is consistent with the subsequent verses about revelation. The term "Najm" is used because the Arabs refer to something that occurs gradually and at different intervals as "Nujuman" (this term is frequently used regarding loan installments and other similar matters). Since the Quran was revealed to the Noble Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) over 23 years and at different stages, it is referred to as "Najm," and the meaning of "when it goes down" (إِذا هَوى) is its descent upon the pure heart of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Another group has considered it a reference to one of the stars in the sky, such as "Pleiades" (Thurayya) [^1] or "Sirius" (Shi'ra) ^2, because these stars possess a special significance.

Some have also said: "An-Najm" refers to the meteors by which devils are driven away from the heavens, and the Arabs call these meteors "Najm."

However, none of these four interpretations has clear evidence; rather, the apparent meaning of the verse, as required by the general use of the word "An-Najm," is an oath by all the stars of the sky, which are—

[^1]: "Thurayya" is a cluster of seven stars, six of which are visible and one is very faint, which is usually used to test people's visual acuity. The oath by this star might be due to its great distance from us. ^2: "Shi'ra" is one of the brightest stars in the sky, about which we will provide more explanation under verse 49 of this Surah, God willing. The secret of the oath by it may be that it is exceptionally brilliant and possesses unique characteristics.

—among the prominent signs of Allah's greatness, the great secrets of the universe, and the exceptionally vast creations of the Lord.

This is not the first time the Quran swears by great entities of creation; in other verses of the Quran, oaths are taken by the sun, the moon, and the like.

The emphasis on their setting, while their rising attracts more attention, is because the setting of stars is evidence of their contingency (being created) and is also a proof for rejecting the belief of star-worshippers, as mentioned in the story of Abraham (عليه السلام): "When the night covered him over, he saw a star: He said: 'Is this my Lord?' But when it set, he said: 'I love not those that set!'" (Al-An'am: 76).

It is also noteworthy that the concept of "rising" is embedded in the linguistic root of "Najm," for as Raghib says in Al-Mufradat, the origin of "Najm" is the "rising celestial body." Because of this, the "sprouting of a plant from the earth," "the emergence of a tooth in the mouth," and "the appearance of an idea in the mind" are referred to as "Najm."

Thus, Allah has sworn by both the rising of the stars and their setting, as both are evidence of their contingency and their captivity within the laws of creation. [^1]

[^1]: If in some traditions "Najm" is interpreted as the person of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and "Hawa" as his descent from the heavens on the night of Ascension, this is in fact part of the inner meanings (Batn) of the verse, not its apparent meaning (Zahir).

But let us see why this oath was taken. The next verse explains: "Your companion (Muhammad) has not strayed, nor has he erred" (ما ضَلَّ صاحِبُكُمْ وَ ما غَوى).

He always walks the path of Truth, and there is not the slightest deviation in his speech or conduct.

The use of the term "Sahib," which means friend and companion, may be an indication that what he says is out of love and compassion for you.

Many commentators have not distinguished between "Dalla" (strayed) and "Ghawa" (erred), considering them as reinforcements of each other. However, some believe there is a difference between the two: "Dalalah" (straying) is when a person finds no path to the destination at all, while "Ghawayah" (erring) is when his path is not straight and is not free from flaws; the first is like "disbelief" (Kufr) and the second is like "transgression and sin" (Fisq).

However, Raghib in Al-Mufradat says regarding the meaning of "Ghayy": "It is ignorance accompanied by a corrupt belief."

Therefore, "Dalalah" means absolute ignorance and unawareness, while "Ghawayah" is ignorance accompanied by a false belief. In any case, Allah intends in this phrase to negate any kind of deviation, ignorance, misguidance, and error from His Prophet, and to neutralize the accusations leveled against him by enemies in this regard.

Then, to emphasize this point and prove that what he says is from Allah, He adds: "Nor does he speak from [his own] inclination" (وَ ما يَنْطِقُ عَنِ الْهَوى).

This expression is like an argument for what appeared in the previous verse regarding the negation of straying and erring, because the source of misguidance is often following one's whims and desires.

In Surah Sad, verse 26, we read: "And do not follow desire, for it will mislead you from the way of Allah."

And in the well-known Hadith of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), it is stated: "As for following whims, it bars one from the Truth." [^1]

Some commentators believe that the phrase "Your companion has not strayed" refers to the negation of madness from the Prophet, and the phrase "nor has he erred" refers to the negation of being a poet or any connection to poetry, as we read in Surah Ash-Shu'ara, verse 224: "And the poets—it is the deviants who follow them" (referring to imaginative and aimless poets). And the phrase "Nor does he speak from inclination" negates the attribution of soothsaying (Kahanah), as soothsayers are people of whims and desires.

Then He says with total clarity: "It is not but a revelation revealed [to him]" (إِنْ هُوَ إِلاَّ وَحْيٌ يُوحى).

He says nothing of his own accord, and the Quran is not a product of his own thought; it is all from Allah. The proof of this claim is inherent within it. An examination of the Quranic verses clearly testifies that no human being, no matter how learned or thoughtful—

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 42.

—let alone an unlettered person raised in an environment filled with ignorance and superstitions, is capable of bringing such profound words that, after centuries, continue to inspire the minds of thinkers and can serve as a foundation for building a righteous, healthy, faithful, and progressive society.

Furthermore, it should be noted that this statement does not only apply to the verses of the Quran; by the context of the preceding verses, it also includes the Sunnah of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), which is also according to divine revelation, for this verse explicitly says: "He does not speak from inclination; whatever he says is revelation."

The following interesting Hadith is another witness to this claim: Suyuti, who is a famous scholar of the Ahl al-Sunnah, narrates in the commentary Al-Durr al-Manthur: "One day the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) ordered all the doors of the houses that opened into the Prophet's Mosque to be closed (except for the door of Ali's (عليه السلام) house). This was hard for the Muslims to bear, to the point that Hamza, the Prophet's uncle, complained, saying: 'How is it that you closed the doors of your uncle, Abu Bakr, Umar, and Abbas, but left the door of your cousin open?' (preferring him over others).

When the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) realized that this was hard for them, he invited the people to the mosque and delivered a unique sermon in praise and monotheism of Allah, then added: 'O people! I did not close them, nor did I open them; I did not expel you, nor did I settle him (Ali). (Whatever happened was divine revelation and the command of Allah).' Then he recited: 'By the star when it goes down... Your companion has not strayed, nor has he erred... Nor does he speak from inclination. It is not but a revelation revealed.'" [^1]

This Hadith, which expresses the high status of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), among the entire Islamic Ummah after the person of the Prophet, shows that not only are the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) words according to revelation, but his actions and conduct are as well.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Durr al-Manthur, Vol. 6, p. 122 (with slight abridgment).

Ayahs

عَلَّمَهُۥ شَدِيدُ ٱلْقُوَىٰ (٥)
taught him by one of great powers, (5)
ذُو مِرَّةٍ فَٱسْتَوَىٰ (٦)
possessed of sound judgement. He settled, (6)
وَهُوَ بِٱلْأُفُقِ ٱلْأَعْلَىٰ (٧)
while he was on the highest horizon. (7)
ثُمَّ دَنَا فَتَدَلَّىٰ (٨)
Then he drew nearer and nearer (8)
فَكَانَ قَابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنَىٰ (٩)
until he was within two bows’ length or even nearer, (9)
فَأَوْحَىٰٓ إِلَىٰ عَبْدِهِۦ مَآ أَوْحَىٰ (١٠)
whereat He revealed to His servant whatever He revealed. (10)
مَا كَذَبَ ٱلْفُؤَادُ مَا رَأَىٰٓ (١١)
The heart did not deny what it saw. (11)
أَفَتُمَـٰرُونَهُۥ عَلَىٰ مَا يَرَىٰ (١٢)
Will you then dispute with him about what he saw?! (12)

Commentary

The First Meeting with the Beloved!

Following the previous verses which spoke of the revelation being sent down to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), these verses speak of the Teacher of the revelation.

However, it must first be noted that these verses are initially shrouded in a halo of ambiguity, which must be examined with great care to resolve. We will first provide a brief commentary on these verses, followed by a detailed analysis.

It says: "He who possesses mighty power has taught the Prophet of Islam" (عَلَّمَهُ شَدِيدُ الْقُوى).

Then, for further emphasis, it adds: "The one who possesses extraordinary strength and authority over all things" (ذُو مِرَّةٍ فَاسْتَوى).

He gave him this teaching "while he was on the highest horizon" (وَ هُوَ بِالْأُفُقِ الْأَعْلى).

"Then he drew near, and came closer" (ثُمَّ دَنا فَتَدَلَّى).

"Until the distance between him and his Teacher was the measure of two bows or even less!" (فَكانَ قابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنى).

"And here, Allah revealed to His servant whatever was to be revealed!" (فَأَوْحى إِلى عَبْدِهِ ما أَوْحى).

"The Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) heart was truthful in what it saw and never lied!" (ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى).

"Do you then dispute with him about what he saw and do not believe?" (أَ فَتُمارُونَهُ عَلى ما يَرى).

In the commentary of these verses, there are two different theories: one is well-known (famous) and the other is less common. However, it is necessary first to define the vocabulary and certain words of the verse before explaining these two views.

"Mirrah" (مِرَّة), as written by many lexicographers and commentators, means "twisted." Since a rope is stronger the better it is twisted, this word is used to mean power, strength, and physical or spiritual firmness. Some have derived it from "murur" meaning "passing through," but this does not align well with what lexicographers have recorded.

"Tadalla" (تَدَلَّى) is derived from the root tadalli (on the weight of tajalli). According to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, it means "drawing near." Therefore, it is an emphasis for the phrase "dana" (دنى) which preceded it, and both carry the same meaning. However, some have distinguished between the two, saying tadalla means dependency, attachment, or hanging, like the attachment of fruit to a tree; hence, fruits hanging from a tree are called "dawali" [1].

"Qab" (قاب) means measure, and "Qaws" (قَوْس) means a bow. Therefore, "Qaba Qawsayn" means "the length of two bows" (the bow being an ancient weapon for archery).

Some have considered qaws to be from the root qiyas (measurement), meaning a "scale" or "measure." Since the Arab measure was the dhira (the distance from the fingertips to the elbow), Qaba Qawsayn would mean "two cubits."

In some lexicons, qab is mentioned with another meaning: the distance between the part of the bow held by the hand and the curved tip of the bow (bows were arc-shaped with two curved ends). Therefore, Qaba Qawsayn would mean the entire curvature of the bow (take note) [2]. Now we return to the two interpretations:

The well-known theory among commentators is that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) teacher was "Gabriel the Trustworthy," the messenger of Allah's revelation, who possessed extraordinary power.

He, who usually appeared to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in the form of a "handsome man" to deliver the divine message, appeared to him in his original form and appearance only twice in the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) entire life:

The first time is what we read in the above verses, where he appeared on the upper horizon (covering the entire East and West, and was so magnificent that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was deeply moved). It was he who drew near to the Prophet to the extent that there was little distance between them, and the expression "two bows' length" is a metaphor for extreme proximity.

The second time was during the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) Ascension (Mi'raj), which is discussed in the upcoming verses, and by Allah's will, we shall speak of it.

Some commentators who have chosen this view have specified that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) first meeting with Gabriel in his original form took place near the Cave of Hira on Mount Nur [1]. However, this interpretation, despite its many supporters, is not without significant issues because:

  1. In the verse "فَأَوْحى إِلى عَبْدِهِ ما أَوْحى" (He revealed to His servant whatever was to be revealed), the referent of the pronouns (especially the pronoun in abdihi - His servant) is certainly Allah. If "Shadid al-Quwa" (The Mighty in Power) refers to Gabriel, then all the pronouns would refer to him. While it is true that external context can suggest this verse is separate from the rest, the disruption of the consistency of the verses and the referent of the pronouns is certainly contrary to the apparent meaning (zahir).

  2. "Shadid al-Quwa" (The Mighty in Power), meaning one whose powers are all extraordinary, is only appropriate for the Pure Essence of the Lord. It is true that in Surah At-Takwir (verse 20), Gabriel is described as "ذِي قُوَّةٍ عِنْدَ ذِي الْعَرْشِ مَكِينٍ" (Possessor of power, established in the presence of the Lord of the Throne), but there is a great difference between "Mighty in Power" (plural and absolute) and "Possessor of power" (singular and indefinite).

  3. In the subsequent verses, it is mentioned that the Prophet saw him near the "Sidrat al-Muntaha" (above the heavens). If Gabriel is intended, he was with the Prophet from the beginning of the journey from the earth and did not only see him at the peak of the heavens—unless it is said he saw him first in human form and then in his original form in the heavens, though there is no evidence for this in the verses.

  4. The expression "allamahu" (He taught him) or its like is never used in the Holy Quran regarding Gabriel. This expression is used very frequently regarding Allah in relation to the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) and other prophets. In other words, Gabriel was not the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) teacher, but rather the intermediary of revelation; his Teacher is only Allah.

  5. It is true that Gabriel is a high-ranking angel, but the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) certainly holds a higher station. As mentioned in the story of the Ascension, Gabriel was in the presence of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) during the upward journey until he reached a point where he stopped and said: "If I go one fingertip higher, my wings will burn!" Yet the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) continued his journey.

Given this, seeing Gabriel in his original form does not match the immense importance given to it in these verses. To put it simply, it was not so monumental for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) to achieve such a meeting, whereas these verses accord extraordinary importance to this encounter.

  1. The phrase "ما كَذَبَ الْفُؤادُ ما رَأى" (The heart did not lie about what it saw) is also evidence of an internal intuition (shuhud) rather than a sensory observation with the eyes regarding Gabriel.

  2. Beyond all this, in numerous narrations from the sources of the Ahl al-Bayt (علیهم السلام), these verses are not interpreted as referring to Gabriel. Instead, the narrations align with the second interpretation, which states that these verses refer to a specific internal intuition of the Pure Essence of Allah that occurred for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) in this scene and was repeated during the Ascension, and the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله) was profoundly affected by the spiritual attraction of this meeting [1].

The late Sheikh Tusi in Al-Amali narrates from Ibn Abbas from the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله): "When I was taken up to the heavens, I drew so near to my Lord, the Mighty and Majestic, that the distance between me and Him was like two bows or even less!" [2]

The late Saduq in Ilal al-Shara'i narrates the same theme from Hisham ibn al-Hakam from Imam Musa ibn Jafar (عليه السلام), who said in a long hadith: "When the Prophet was taken on the Night Journey and his distance from the Holy Presence of his Lord was like two bows or less, one of the veils among His veils was lifted from before his eyes!" [3]

In Tafsir Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is also stated: "Then he drew near, meaning the Messenger of Allah drew near to his Lord, the Exalted!" [1]

This meaning appears in numerous other narrations, and it is impossible to ignore all these reports.

In Sunni narrations as well, a report in Durr al-Manthur conveys this same meaning from Ibn Abbas through two chains [2].

The sum of these evidences leads us to choose the second interpretation, which states that "Shadid al-Quwa" refers to Allah, and the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) drawing near was toward His Pure Essence.

It seems that what caused most commentators to turn away from this interpretation and seek the first one is that this interpretation carries a scent of anthropomorphism (tajsim) and implying a physical location for Allah. However, it is certain that He has neither a location nor a body: "Vision perceives Him not, but He perceives all vision" (Al-An'am: 103); "So wherever you turn, there is the Face of Allah" (Al-Baqarah: 115); "And He is with you wherever you are" (Al-Hadid: 4).

Perhaps the sum of these issues also caused some commentators to express their inability to interpret these verses, saying they are among the secrets of the Unseen hidden from us all! They say: one scholar was asked about the interpretation of these verses and he replied: "Where Gabriel becomes powerless, who am I to be able to comprehend this meaning!" [3]

However, considering that the Quran is a book of guidance and was revealed for human reflection, contemplation, and remembrance, accepting such a conclusion is also difficult.

But if we interpret the verses according to the second meaning—that is, a type of spiritual intuition and a specific spiritual proximity—all these problems will be resolved.

To explain: undoubtedly, sensory vision regarding Allah is impossible, neither in this world nor in the hereafter, because it would necessitate corporeality and materiality. It would also necessitate change, transformation, perishability, and the need for time and space, and the Essence of the Necessary Being is free from all such things.

However, Allah can be observed through the vision of the heart and the intellect. This is exactly what the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), pointed to in response to Dhi'lab al-Yamani: "Eyes do not perceive Him through physical sight, but hearts perceive Him through the realities of faith." [1]

But it must be noted that "internal vision" is of two kinds: one is "intellectual vision" obtained through reasoning, and the other is the station of "heartfelt intuition" (shuhud), which is a perception above the perception of the intellect and a vision beyond its vision.

This is a station that should not be called the station of "reasoning," but rather the station of "observation"—but an observation with the heart and through the inner self. This is a station that occurs for the "Friends of Allah" (Awliya Allah) with varying degrees and ranks, for internal intuition also has many levels. Of course, understanding its reality is difficult for those who have not reached it.

From the above verses, considering the evidences mentioned, it can be inferred that while the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) possessed the station of intuition, twice during his blessed life he soared so high that he attained the station of "perfect intuition."

One was likely at the beginning of his mission and the other during the Ascension. He drew so near to Allah and stepped upon the carpet of His proximity that many distances and veils were removed—a station that even Gabriel the Trustworthy, the closest of Allah's angels, was unable to reach.

It is clear that expressions like "فَكانَ قابَ قَوْسَيْنِ أَوْ أَدْنى" (the measure of two bows or less) are all metaphorical and express the intensity of proximity. Otherwise, He has no spatial distance from His servants to be measured with "bows" or "cubits." Likewise, the "vision" intended in these verses is not vision with the eyes, but rather that same internal intuition.

In previous discussions regarding the interpretation of "Liqa Allah" (Meeting the Lord), which is repeatedly emphasized in various verses of the Quran as one of the characteristics of the Day of Judgment, we have said that this meeting—contrary to what some narrow-minded people have imagined—is not a sensory meeting or physical observation, but a type of internal intuition, albeit at lower levels, and it never reaches the level of the intuition of the saints and prophets, let alone the level of the perfect intuition of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) on the Night of Ascension.

With this explanation, the objections that seem to surround this interpretation will be resolved. And if some expressions seem to deviate from the literal sense due to the limitations of our language and words in describing these meta-physical matters, it is negligible compared to the objections facing the first interpretation.

Given what has been said, let us review the verses under discussion once more and examine their content from this perspective:

According to this interpretation, the Quran describes the descent of revelation upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) as follows: Allah, the Mighty in Power and All-Powerful, taught him, while he (the Prophet) attained perfection and balance and stood on the highest horizon [1]. Then he drew near, and came closer, such that the distance between him and his Lord was no more than two bows' length, and it was here that Allah revealed to His servant whatever was to be revealed.

And since this internal intuition was difficult for some to accept, He emphasizes that the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) heart saw what it saw in truth and reality, and you should not dispute with him regarding this.

As we have said, interpreting these verses as the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) internal intuition of Allah is more accurate, more consistent with Islamic narrations, a higher virtue for the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), and a more subtle concept (and Allah knows best the realities of matters) [2]. We conclude this discussion with a hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and a hadith from Ali (عليه السلام).

The Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was asked: "Hal ra'ayta Rabbaka?" (Have you ever seen your Lord?). He replied: "Ra'aytuhu bi-fu'adi" (I have seen Him with the eye of my heart!) [3].

And in Nahj al-Balagha, at the beginning of the same sermon of Dhi'lab al-Yamani, it is mentioned that he asked the Imam: "Hal ra'ayta Rabbaka ya Amir al-Mu'minin?" (Have you ever seen your Lord, O Commander of the Faithful?).

He replied: "A-fa-a'budu ma la ara?" (Should I worship one whom I do not see?!). Then he proceeded to the explanation we previously quoted, which refers to "internal intuition" [1].
