Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ رَءَاهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرَىٰ (١٣)
Certainly he saw it yet another time, (13)
عِندَ سِدْرَةِ ٱلْمُنتَهَىٰ (١٤)
by the Lote Tree of the Ultimate Boundary, (14)
عِندَهَا جَنَّةُ ٱلْمَأْوَىٰٓ (١٥)
near which is the Garden of the Abode, (15)
إِذْ يَغْشَى ٱلسِّدْرَةَ مَا يَغْشَىٰ (١٦)
when there covered the Lote Tree what covered it. (16)
مَا زَاغَ ٱلْبَصَرُ وَمَا طَغَىٰ (١٧)
His gaze did not swerve, nor did it overstep the bounds. (17)
لَقَدْ رَأَىٰ مِنْ ءَايَـٰتِ رَبِّهِ ٱلْكُبْرَىٰٓ (١٨)
Certainly he saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord. (18)

Commentary

The Second Encounter!

These verses continue the discussion of the previous verses regarding the issue of revelation, the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله connection with Allah, and his intuitive vision.

It says: "And indeed, he saw him another time" (وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرَى).

"And this vision occurred near the 'Lote Tree of the Utmost Boundary'" (عِنْدَ سِدْرَةِ الْمُنْتَهَى).

"Near which is the Garden of Abode and the supreme Paradise" (عِنْدَهَا جَنَّةُ الْمَأْوَى).

"When there covered the Lote Tree that which covered it" (إِذْ يَغْشَى السِّدْرَةَ مَا يَغْشَى).

These were realities that the Prophet observed, "and his sight did not swerve, nor did it transgress," and he did not see false imaginations in the guise of truth (مَا زَاغَ الْبَصَرُ وَمَا طَغَى).

"He certainly saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord there" (لَقَدْ رَأَى مِنْ آيَاتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرَى).

As we see, the same ambiguity that initially surrounded the previous verses overshadows these verses, which are another segment of the same subject matter. To clarify the content of these verses, we must first examine their individual terms and then consider the whole:

"Nazlah" (نَزْلَة) means descending once; therefore, "Nazlatan ukhra" (نَزْلَةً أُخْرَى) means "in another descent." [^1] From this expression, it is understood that "descending" occurred twice, and this event pertains to the second descent.

"Sidrah" (سِدْرَة) (on the weight of hirfah) according to what most commentators and lexicographers have stated, is a tree with many leaves and much shade. The expression "Sidrat al-Muntaha" (سِدْرَةِ الْمُنْتَهَى) refers to a leafy, shady tree located at the highest point of the heavens, at the ultimate boundary of the ascension of angels, the souls of martyrs, the knowledge of prophets, and the deeds of humans—a place where the angels of the Lord do not pass beyond, and where even Jibril عليه السلام stopped during the journey of the Mi'raj (Ascension) when he reached it.

Regarding the "Sidrat al-Muntaha," although no explanation is provided in the Holy Quran, various descriptions have appeared in Islamic reports and traditions. All of these indicate that the choice of this expression is a type of simile, used because of the limitation and inadequacy of our language to express such great realities.

In a Hadith narrated from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, he said: "I saw on every leaf of its leaves an angel standing, glorifying Allah the Exalted." [^2] In another Hadith narrated from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: "I reached the Sidrat al-Muntaha, and behold, one of its leaves shades a nation among nations."

[^1]: Some lexicographers and commentators have interpreted "Nazlah" to mean "once" (marrah); thus, the meaning of descent is not in it, and "Nazlatan ukhra" would only mean "one more time." However, it is not clear why they abandoned the original root of "Nazlah" here, while others preserved it and interpreted it as we mentioned above (reflect on this).

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, under the verses being discussed.

These expressions show that what is meant is never a tree similar to what we see on earth; rather, it refers to a magnificent canopy in the proximity of the Mercy of the Truth, where angels glorify Him upon its leaves, and nations of the good and pure reside in its shade.

"Jannat al-Ma'wa" (جَنَّةُ الْمَأْوَى) means the Paradise that is a place of residence. [^2] There is discussion among commentators as to which Paradise this is. Some consider it to be the Eternal Paradise (Jannat al-Khuld) that awaits all people of faith and the God-fearing, and they believe its location is in the heavens, citing verse 19 of Surah as-Sajdah as evidence: "For those who believe and do righteous deeds, for them are the Gardens of Abode as hospitality for what they used to do," because this verse, by the context of the verse following it, certainly speaks of the Eternal Paradise.

However, since we read elsewhere: "And hasten to forgiveness from your Lord and a Garden whose width is the heavens and the earth" (Al-Imran: 133), some have considered this meaning unlikely, because the apparent sense of the verses under discussion indicates that "Jannat al-Ma'wa" is in the heaven and is distinct from the Eternal Paradise whose width is as vast as all the heavens and the earth.

[^1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 155. [^2]: "Ma'wa" originally means a place of joining/gathering, and since the residence of individuals in one place causes them to be joined together, this word is applied to a place of residence.

Therefore, it has sometimes been interpreted as a special station within Paradise located next to the "Sidrat al-Muntaha," reserved for the elite and the sincere.

And sometimes it is said to mean the "Barzakh Paradise" where the souls of martyrs and believers go temporarily.

The latter interpretation seems most appropriate. Among the things that clearly testify to it is that in many narrations of the Mi'raj, it is mentioned that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw a group of people enjoying blessings in this Paradise, while we know that no one enters the Eternal Paradise before the Day of Resurrection. The verses of the Quran clearly indicate that the God-fearing enter Paradise on the Day of Judgment after the reckoning, not immediately after death, and the souls of martyrs are also in the Barzakh Paradise, as they too do not enter the Eternal Paradise before the Resurrection.

The verse "His sight did not swerve, nor did it transgress" (مَا زَاغَ الْبَصَرُ وَمَا طَغَى) refers to the fact that the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله eye, in its observation, did not turn left or right, nor did it exceed the limit or the destination; what he saw was the very reality itself. (Because "zagha" is from the root "zaygh," meaning deviation to the left or right, and "tagha" is from the root "tughyan," meaning exceeding the limit).

In other words, a human falls into error when observing something if they do not focus precisely on the object itself, or if they look to the left, the right, or beyond it. [^1]

[^1]: In the commentary "Al-Mizan," the first word is interpreted as "error in observing the quality of a thing," and the second word as "error in the essence of seeing," but no clear reason is given for this distinction; rather, what is found in the lexicon is the interpretation we mentioned above.

Now that we have finished interpreting the individual terms of the verse, we turn to the collective interpretation of the verses.

Here again, the same two views that existed in the interpretation of the previous verses are followed.

A large group of commentators consider the verses to refer to the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله meeting with Jibril عليه السلام for the second time in his original form. They say the meaning is that once again, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, during his descent from the Mi'raj, saw Jibril in his original form near the "Sidrat al-Muntaha," and his eye never fell into error while observing this scene.

The Prophet observed some of the great signs of the Truth here, which refers either to the real form of Jibril, or some of the signs of the greatness of the heavens and their wonders, or both.

However, the objections we previously mentioned for this interpretation still remain, and indeed, some other objections are added to it, including:

The expression "Nazlatan ukhra" (in another descent) does not have a clear meaning according to this interpretation. But according to the second interpretation, the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, in "another intuitive vision" during the Mi'raj above the heavens, observed the Pure Essence of God. In other words, Allah descended once more upon his pure heart (نَزْلَةً أُخْرَى) and a complete vision was realized, at the place which is the ultimate point of proximity to Allah for servants, near the Sidrat al-Muntaha, where the "Jannat al-Ma'wa" is located, while the "Sidrat al-Muntaha" was covered by veils of light.

The eye of the Prophet’s صلى الله عليه وآله heart in this vision never fell upon anything other than the Truth, and he saw none but Him. It was there that he also observed the signs of Allah's greatness in the horizons and within souls.

The issue of intuitive vision (shuhud batini), as we previously mentioned, is a type of perception and sight that resembles neither "intellectual perceptions" nor "sensory perceptions" that a human perceives through the external senses. In some respects, it can be likened to a human's knowledge of their own existence and their own thoughts and imaginations.

To explain: we are certain of our own existence, we perceive our own thoughts, and we are aware of our will, decisions, and inclinations. However, this awareness was not obtained through reasoning, nor through external observation; rather, it is a type of intuitive vision for us through which we are aware of our existence and our spiritual states.

For this reason, the knowledge obtained from intuitive vision is not subject to any error, because it is not through reasoning such that an error could occur in its premises, nor is it through the senses such that an error could enter through them.

It is true that we cannot comprehend the reality of the vision that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله attained regarding Allah on that historic night of the Mi'raj, but the example we gave is suitable for bringing the idea closer, and Islamic narrations also serve as our guide.

Key Points

1- The Mi'raj is an Established Reality

Among Islamic scholars, there is no dispute regarding the essence of the Mi'raj, as both the verses of the Quran here and at the beginning of Surah al-Isra' testify to it, as do mutawatir (frequently narrated) traditions. However, some who, due to certain preconceptions, could not accept that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله flew to the heavens with his "body" and "soul," have interpreted it as a "spiritual Mi'raj" and something similar to a dream state! Yet, this physical flight of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله presents neither an intellectual problem nor a problem from the perspective of modern sciences, the details of which we have explained extensively in the commentary of Surah al-Isra'. [^1] Therefore, there is no reason for us to abandon the apparent meaning of the verses and the explicit traditions because of perceived improbability.

Furthermore, the expressions in the above verses show that a group had risen to dispute this matter. History also says: the issue of the Mi'raj sparked an uproar among the opponents. If the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله had claimed a spiritual Mi'raj or something like a dream, this uproar would have had no basis in improbability.

2- The Goal of the Mi'raj

The goal was for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to attain intuitive vision on one hand, and to see the greatness of Allah in the expanse of the heavens with his very physical eyes on the other, which is referred to both in the last verse under discussion here (لَقَدْ رَأَى مِنْ آيَاتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرَى) and in verse 1 of Surah al-Isra' (لِنُرِيَهُ مِنْ آيَاتِنَا). He also became aware of many important matters regarding the angels, the people of Paradise, the people of Hell, and the souls of the prophets, which served as his inspiration in teaching and training Allah's creation throughout his blessed life.

3- The Mi'raj and Paradise

From the verses under discussion, it is understood that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله passed by Paradise on the night of the Mi'raj, or entered it. Whether this Paradise is the Eternal Paradise (Jannat al-Khuld), as a group of commentators have said, or the "Barzakh Paradise" which we have chosen, in any case, he observed important matters regarding the future of humans in this Paradise, the details of which have come in Islamic reports, and we will refer to a part of them, Allah willing.

4- The Mi'raj in Islamic Narrations

Among the issues that have caused some to question the narrations of the Mi'raj, or even the entire event, is the presence of some weak and fabricated narrations interspersed among them. However, as the famous commentator, the late al-Tabarsi, stated in "Majma' al-Bayan," the narrations of the Mi'raj can be divided into four groups:

A- Narrations that are certain due to being mutawatir (such as the essence of the Mi'raj).

B- Narrations that (are narrated from reliable sources) and contain matters for which there is no intellectual obstacle to acceptance, such as narrations that speak of observing many signs of Allah's greatness in the heavens.

C- Narrations whose apparent meaning conflicts with the principles we have from the verses of the Quran and established Islamic traditions, but which are nevertheless capable of interpretation, such as narrations saying the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw a group of Paradise-dwellers in Paradise and a group of Hell-dwellers in Hell (where it must be said that what is meant is the Barzakh Paradise and Hell, where the souls of believers and martyrs are in one, and the souls of disbelievers and criminals are in the other). [^1]

[^1]: Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 12, p. 17 onwards. [^1]: In some verses of the Quran, it is mentioned that the God-fearing will enter Paradise in groups on the Day of Resurrection, and the disbelievers will enter Hell in groups (Surah az-Zumar, verses 71 and 73). Other verses also testify to this meaning (such as verse 70 of az-Zukhruf, 85-86 of Maryam, 47 of ad-Dukhan).

D- Narrations that contain false and baseless content that is in no way acceptable, and whose content testifies to their being fabricated, such as narrations saying the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله saw Allah with his "physical eye," or spoke to Him and observed Him; such narrations and their like are certainly fabricated (unless they are interpreted as intuitive vision).

With this classification in mind, we turn to a brief review of the Mi'raj narrations:

From the sum of the narrations, it is well understood that the noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله traversed this heavenly journey in several stages.

The first stage was the distance between Masjid al-Haram and Masjid al-Aqsa, which is mentioned in the first verse of Surah al-Isra': "Exalted is He who took His servant by night from Masjid al-Haram to Masjid al-Aqsa."

According to some reliable narrations, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, accompanied by Jibril عليه السلام, descended in the land of Medina along the way and prayed there, [^2] and also prayed in "Masjid al-Aqsa" in the presence of the souls of great prophets like Ibrahim, Musa, and 'Isa عليهم السلام, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was the Imam of the congregation. Then from there, the heavenly journey of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله began, and he traversed the seven heavens [^1] one after another. In each heaven, he encountered new scenes, meeting prophets and angels, and in some heavens, he encountered Hell or its inhabitants, and in others, Paradise and its inhabitants. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله stored precious and very instructive memories from each of them in his pure soul and observed wonders, each of which was a symbol and a secret among the secrets of the universe. After returning, he would explain these clearly, and sometimes in the language of metaphor and parable, for the awareness of the Ummah at appropriate opportunities, using them extensively for teaching and training.

This indicates that one of the important goals of this heavenly journey was to utilize the spiritual and educational results of these invaluable observations. The meaningful Quranic expression "He certainly saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord" in the verses under discussion can be a brief and concise reference to all these matters. Of course, as we said, the Paradise and Hell that the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله observed during the Mi'raj journey, and those he saw being blessed or punished therein, were not the Paradise and Hell of the Resurrection, but rather the Barzakh Paradise and Hell, because according to the verses we previously referred to, the Holy Quran says the Paradise and Hell of the Resurrection are granted to the righteous and the wicked after the Day of Judgment and the completion of the reckoning.

Finally, he reached the seventh heaven, and there he observed veils of light, where the "Sidrat al-Muntaha" and "Jannat al-Ma'wa" were located. In that world of total light and radiance, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله reached the peak of intuitive vision, proximity to Allah, and the station of "two bows' length or nearer." During this journey, Allah addressed him and gave him very important instructions and many words, a collection of which remains for us today in Islamic traditions in the form of "Hadith Qudsi," and in the next section, Allah willing, we will refer to a part of it.

It is noteworthy that according to the explicit statement of many narrations, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله suddenly observed Ali عليه السلام beside him in various parts of this great journey, and expressions are seen in these narrations that testify to the extraordinary greatness of Ali's عليه السلام station after the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله.

Despite all this, the narrations of the Mi'raj contain complex and mysterious sentences whose content is not easy to uncover, and they are, so to speak, among the "mutashabih" (ambiguous) narrations—that is, narrations whose explanation must be left to the Infallibles عليهم السلام themselves.

(For more information on the Mi'raj narrations, refer to Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 18, pp. 282 to 410).

Additionally, the narrations of the Mi'raj are also extensively mentioned in the books of the Ahl al-Sunnah, and about 30 of their narrators have transmitted the Hadith of the Mi'raj. [^1] Here the question arises: how did all this long travel, these strange and diverse events, all these long conversations, and all these observations occur in one night or less than one night?! However, with attention to one point, the answer to this question becomes clear: the Mi'raj journey was never an ordinary journey to be measured by ordinary standards. Neither the essence of the journey was ordinary, nor its mount; neither its observations were ordinary, nor its conversations. The scales used in it are not like the limited and small scales of our earthly globe, nor do the similes mentioned in it fully express the greatness of the scenes the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله observed. Everything occurred in a supernatural manner and on scales outside the space and time with which we are familiar and accustomed.

Therefore, it is not surprising that these matters, by the time scale of our planet Earth, took place in one night or less than one night (reflect on this).

[^2]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 18, p. 319. [^1]: According to some verses of the Quran like "Indeed, We have adorned the nearest heaven with an adornment of stars" (as-Saffat: 6), all that we see of the upper world and all the stars and galaxies are all part of the first heaven; therefore, the other six heavens are worlds beyond them. [^1]: Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 13, p. 29 (under the first verses of Surah al-Isra'—narrative discussion).

5- A Glimpse of Allah's Conversations with His Prophet on the Night of the Mi'raj:

In the books of Hadith, a narration from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, from the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله has come regarding this matter, which is very detailed and long, and we bring glimpses of it here—content that shows what the axis of the conversations was on that historic night, and how they soared like the heights of the heavens.

At the beginning of the Hadith, we read that on the night of the Mi'raj, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله asked the Sublimely Purified Lord: "O Lord, which deed is most excellent?"

Allah the Exalted said: "Nothing is more excellent to Me than reliance on Me, and contentment with what I have apportioned. O Muhammad! My love is mandatory for those who love one another for My sake, My love is mandatory for those who are compassionate for My sake, My love is mandatory for those who maintain ties for My sake, and My love is mandatory for those who rely on Me. And for My love, there is no sign, no goal, and no end!"

In this way, the conversations begin with love—a love that is infinite and vast, and fundamentally, the universe revolves around this axis of love.

In another segment, it says: "O Ahmad [^1]! Do not be like children who love green and yellow and glitz and glamour, and when they are given sweet and pleasant food, they become arrogant and consign everything to oblivion."

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله here submitted: "O Lord, guide me to a deed that brings proximity to Your presence."

He said: "Make the night day, and the day night!" He asked: "How?!" He said: "Make it so that your sleep is prayer, and never fill your stomach completely."

In another segment, it says: "O Ahmad! My love is the love of the poor and the deprived. Draw near to them, and sit in their gatherings, so that I may draw near to you. And keep the world-worshipping wealthy far from you, and beware of their gatherings!"

[^1]: It is noteworthy that the Prophet's (ص) name in this Hadith is mentioned everywhere as "Ahmad" except at the beginning where it is "Muhammad." Yes, Muhammad was his earthly name, and Ahmad was his heavenly name. Why should it not be so, while "Ahmad," by virtue of being a "superlative noun," expresses greater praiseworthiness? On that historic night, and at that stage of proximity to God, the Prophet (ص) must pass from "Muhammad" and become "Ahmad," especially since the distance between "Ahmad" and "Ahad" (The One) is less!

In another segment, He says: "O Ahmad! Hate the glitz of the world and the world-worshippers, and love the Hereafter and the people of the Hereafter."

He asks: "O Lord! Who are the people of the world and the Hereafter?"

He said: "The people of the world are those who eat much, laugh much, sleep much, and get angry much, and are rarely satisfied. They neither apologize to anyone for their evils, nor do they accept an apology if someone seeks it from them. They are lazy in obeying Allah and brave in committing sins. They have long-reaching hopes, and while their appointed time (death) has drawn near, they never reckon their own deeds. Their benefit to people is little; they are talkative individuals, lacking a sense of responsibility, and are fond of eating and drinking.

The people of the world neither give thanks to Allah in blessing, nor are they patient in calamities. Great services are small in their eyes (and their own small services are great!). They praise themselves for performing a task they have not done, and they demand something that is not their right. They constantly speak of their own desires and point out the faults of people while hiding their virtues!"

He asked: "O Lord? Do the world-worshippers have any faults other than these?"

He said: "O Ahmad! Their fault is that ignorance and folly are abundant in them. They do not show humility to the teacher from whom they learned knowledge, and they consider themselves wise, but in the eyes of the aware, they are ignorant and foolish."

Then He turned to the descriptions of the people of the Hereafter and the inhabitants of Paradise, continuing thus: "They are people of modesty; their ignorance is little, their benefits are many. People are at ease from them, while they themselves are in toil from their own souls, and their words are measured. They are constantly reckoning their own deeds, and for this reason, they put themselves to trouble. Their eyes go to sleep, but their hearts are awake; their eyes weep, and their hearts are constantly in remembrance of Allah. When people are recorded among the heedless, they are recorded among the rememberers (dhakirin). At the beginning of blessings, they praise Allah, and at the end, they give Him thanks. Their prayer is answered in the presence of Allah, and their request is heard, and the angels are delighted by their existence... (Heedless) people are dead to them, and Allah is to them the Living, the Sustainer, and the Generous (their ambition is so high that they look to none but Him)... People die once in their lives, but they, because of the struggle with the self and opposing desires, die seventy times every day (and find new life)!... When they stand before Me for worship, they are like a solid structure and a steel dam, and in their hearts, there is no attention to created beings. By My Might and Majesty, I shall grant them a pure life, and at the end of their lives, I Myself shall take their souls, and I shall open the gates of heaven for the flight of their souls. I shall remove all veils from before them, and I shall command Paradise to adorn itself for them!... O Ahmad! Worship has ten parts, nine of which are seeking the lawful (halal). When your food and drink are lawful, you are in My protection and guardianship..."

And in another segment, it says: "O Ahmad! Do you know which life is more pleasant and more enduring?"

He said: "O Allah, no!" He said: "The pleasant life is that in which its possessor does not remain heedless of My remembrance for a moment, does not forget My blessing, is not unaware of My right, and seeks My pleasure night and day. But the enduring life is that in which one acts for their own salvation, and the world is small in their eyes, and the Hereafter is great. They prefer My pleasure over their own pleasure and constantly seek My satisfaction. They hold My right as great and pay attention to My awareness of them. In the face of every sin and disobedience, they remember Me, and they purify their heart from what I dislike. They hate Satan and satanic whisperings and do not allow Iblis to dominate their heart or give him a way. When they do so, I place a special love in their heart, such that their entire heart will be in My possession, and their leisure, occupation, concern, grief, and speech will be from the gifts I bestow upon the people of My love! I open the eyes and ears of their heart, so that they may hear the realities of the unseen with the ear of their heart and behold My Majesty and Greatness with their heart!"

Finally, this luminous Hadith ends with these awakening sentences: "O Ahmad! If a servant performs the prayer of all the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth, and performs the fasting of all the inhabitants of the heavens and the earth, and like the angels does not eat food and does not wear (luxurious) clothing (and lives in the ultimate asceticism and detachment), but in their heart there is an atom's weight of world-worship or seeking leadership or love for the adornment of the world, they shall not be in My proximity in My eternal abode! And I shall strip My love from their heart! Peace and My mercy be upon you, and praise be to Allah, Lord of the worlds." [^1]

These celestial words, which take the human soul with them to the heights of the heavens, and guide it through the divine Mi'raj and draw it to the threshold of love and vision, are only a part of the Hadith Qudsi.

Furthermore, we are certain that besides what the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله recounted for us in his words, there were secrets, conversations, symbols, and signs exchanged between him and his Beloved on that night of love and longing, attraction and union, which neither ears have the capacity to hear nor ordinary thoughts the power to comprehend. For this reason, they have remained hidden within the pure soul of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله forever, and none but his elite have become aware of them.

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 77, pp. 21 to 30 (summarized).

Ayahs

أَفَرَءَيْتُمُ ٱللَّـٰتَ وَٱلْعُزَّىٰ (١٩)
Have you considered Lat and ‘Uzza? (19)
وَمَنَوٰةَ ٱلثَّالِثَةَ ٱلْأُخْرَىٰٓ (٢٠)
and Manat, the third one? (20)
أَلَكُمُ ٱلذَّكَرُ وَلَهُ ٱلْأُنثَىٰ (٢١)
Are you to have males and He females? (21)
تِلْكَ إِذًا قِسْمَةٌ ضِيزَىٰٓ (٢٢)
That, then, will be an unfair division! (22)
إِنْ هِىَ إِلَّآ أَسْمَآءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوهَآ أَنتُمْ وَءَابَآؤُكُم مَّآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ بِهَا مِن سُلْطَـٰنٍ ۚ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ وَمَا تَهْوَى ٱلْأَنفُسُ ۖ وَلَقَدْ جَآءَهُم مِّن رَّبِّهِمُ ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ (٢٣)
These are but names, which you have coined—you and your fathers—for which Allah has not sent down any authority. They follow nothing but conjectures and the desires of the [lower] soul, while there has already come to them the guidance from their Lord. (23)

Commentary

These Idols are the Offspring of Your Desires

Following the discussions regarding "Monotheism," "Revelation," the "Ascension (Mi'raj)," and the "signs of the greatness of the One God in the heavens," the text now addresses the falsehood of polytheism and the superstitious beliefs of the polytheists concerning idols.

It says: "Now that you have recognized the greatness of Allah and His mighty signs, tell Me about the idols 'al-Lat' and 'al-Uzza'..." (أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ اللَّاتَ وَالْعُزَّى).

"And also 'Manat,' the third one, the other, which you mention following them; are these the likenesses of the daughters of God, and are they the source of profit or loss for you?!" (وَمَنَاةَ الثَّالِثَةَ الْأُخْرَى) [1]

"Is the male for you and the female for Him?!" (أَلَكُمُ الذَّكَرُ وَلَهُ الْأُنْثَى). While according to your own assumption, daughters are of less value than sons, and even when

[1]: Regarding these three idols, we will speak in detail, God willing, in the "Key Points" section. What is noteworthy here is the expression "the third" (al-thalithah) and "the other" (al-ukhra) used for the idol Manat. Many interpretations have been mentioned for these two terms, most of which are baseless affectations. What seems more appropriate is that the importance of these idols among the Arab polytheists followed the order mentioned in the verse; thus, "Manat" held the third rank, and its description as "the other" is due to its later status and position.

you hear that your wife has given birth to a daughter, your faces turn black with intense grief and rage!

"If it be so, this is an unfair division that you have made between yourselves and God!" (تِلْكَ إِذًا قِسْمَةٌ ضِيزَى) [1] because you consider God's share to be inferior to your own! In this way, the Quran mocks their debased and superstitious thoughts: on one hand, you bury daughters alive and consider them a disgrace, and on the other hand, you consider the angels to be the daughters of God. Not only do you worship the angels themselves, but even their lifeless statues in the form of idols hold such respect in your eyes that you prostrate before them, seek refuge in them during hardships, and ask them for your needs. Truly, it is ridiculous and shameful! From this, it becomes clear that at least many of the stone and wooden idols worshipped by the Arabs were, in their view, statues of angels—angels whom they considered "Lords of the Species" (Rabb al-Naw') and the managers and directors of the universe, and they perceived their relationship with God as that of daughter and father.

When these superstitions are placed alongside their other superstition regarding daughters, the strange contradiction itself is the best evidence of the groundlessness of their beliefs and thoughts. How remarkable it is that in a few short sentences, the Quran draws a line of cancellation through all of them and reveals their absurdity.

From this, it is understood that the Quran never intends to validate the belief of the Pre-Islamic Arabs regarding the difference between girls and boys; rather, it seeks to confront the opponent with their own accepted premises—what is termed "Dialectic" (Jadal) in logic. Otherwise, in the logic of Islam, neither do girls and boys differ in terms of human value, nor do angels have gender, nor are they children of God, nor does God have any children at all. These are baseless hypotheses built upon other baseless hypotheses, but they serve as the best response to prove the weakness of the thought and logic of these superstition-worshippers.

In the final verse under discussion, the Quran states with decisiveness: "These are nothing but names which you and your fathers have named (contentless names and titles without reality), for which Allah has sent down no authority or proof" (إِنْ هِيَ إِلَّا أَسْمَاءٌ سَمَّيْتُمُوهَا أَنْتُمْ وَآبَاؤُكُمْ مَا أَنْزَلَ اللَّهُ بِهَا مِنْ سُلْطَانٍ) [1]. You have no rational proof for it, nor any proof through Divine revelation; it is nothing but a handful of illusions, superstitions, and hollow words.

And at the end, it adds: "They follow nothing but baseless conjectures and what their souls desire," and these delusions are all born of imagination and passion (إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَمَا تَهْوَى الْأَنْفُسُ) [2] "while guidance has surely come to them from their Lord" (وَلَقَدْ جَاءَهُمْ مِنْ رَبِّهِمُ الْهُدَى).

But they close their eyes and turn their backs to it, plunging into the darkness of these illusions.

[1]: "Sultan" means authority and dominance, and vivid, convincing proofs are called "Sultan" because they are the means of prevailing over an opponent.

[2]: The word "ma" in "ma tahwa al-anfus" is a relative pronoun (mawsulah); the possibility has also been raised that it is an infinitive particle (masdariyah), though the result does not differ much.

Key Points

1- The Three Famous Idols of the Arabs

The Arab polytheists had many idols, but among them, the three idols "al-Lat," "al-Uzza," and "al-Manat" undoubtedly held special importance and fame.

There is discussion and disagreement regarding the naming of these three idols, their original maker, and the location and tribe that worshipped them. We shall suffice here with what is mentioned in the book Bulugh al-Arab fi Ma'rifat Ahwal al-Arab:

"The first famous idol chosen by the Arabs was 'Manat,' which was made after 'Amr ibn Luhay' transferred idol-worship from Syria to the Hijaz. This idol was located in an area by the Red Sea, between Medina and Mecca, and all Arabs held it in high regard and offered sacrifices to it. However, the two tribes of 'Aws' and 'Khazraj' valued it more than anyone else, until the eighth year of the Hijrah—the year of the 'Conquest of Mecca'—when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was traveling from Medina to Mecca, he sent the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, who broke it into pieces.

Sometime after making the idol Manat, the Pre-Islamic Arabs turned to 'al-Lat,' which was a square-shaped stone located in the land of Ta'if, at the very spot where the minaret on the left side of the Ta'if mosque stands today. The attendants of this idol were mostly from the 'Thaqif' tribe. When they became Muslim, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent 'Mughirah,' who broke it and burned it in fire.

The third idol chosen by the Pre-Islamic Arabs was 'al-Uzza,' located in a place on the route from Mecca to Iraq near 'Dhat Irq,' and the Quraysh held it in great importance.

They valued these three idols to such an extent that during the circumambulation (Tawaf) of the House of Allah, they would say: 'By al-Lat, al-Uzza, and Manat the third, the other; for they are the high-soaring birds (al-gharanique al-ula), and indeed their intercession is hoped for!' They perceived them as the daughters of God (it appears they viewed them as likenesses of angels whom they called the daughters of God) [1]. Strangely, in naming them, they mostly used the names of Allah, but with feminine endings to reflect the aforementioned belief. Thus, 'al-Lat' was originally 'al-Ilahah,' then the letter 'h' was dropped to become 'al-Lat' [2]. 'Al-Uzza' is the feminine form of 'A'azz' (the Most Mighty), and 'Manat' is derived from 'mana Allahu al-shay',' meaning the decreeing of something by God.

Some also consider it derived from the root 'naw',' referring to the stars that the Arabs believed brought rain upon their rising. Others consider it from the root 'mana' (rhyming with 'sa'a'), meaning the shedding of blood, because the blood of sacrifices was poured beside it [3]. In any case, the Arabs respected these idols to such an extent that they chose names

[1]: Bulugh al-Arab fi Ma'rifat Ahwal al-Arab, Vol. 2, pp. 202-203.

[2]: The word "al-Lat" according to this meaning should be written as "al-Latah" (with a ta marbuta), but because in the state of pausing (waqf) it turns into an "h" and might be confused with "Allah," it was written as "al-Lat."

[3]: The first possibility is mentioned in Al-Kashshaf and the second in Bulugh al-Arab.

such as 'Abd al-Uzza' and 'Abd Manat' for individuals or for certain tribes [1].

2- Names Without Reality!

One of the oldest sources of polytheism and dualism or pluralism is the diversity of beings in the world. Since narrow-minded individuals could not believe that all these various and diverse beings in the heavens and the earth were created by the One God (as they used themselves as a measure, having mastery and skill in only one or a limited number of tasks), they posited a god for every type of being, which they referred to as the "Lord of the Species" (Rabb al-Naw'). For example: the Lord of the Sea, the Lord of the Desert, the Lord of Rain, the Lord of the Sun, the Lord of War, and the Lord of Peace! These imaginary gods, whom they sometimes also referred to as angels, were in their belief the rulers of this world. Whenever a problem arose in any area, they would seek refuge in its respective "Lord." Then, since these "Lords" were not perceptible beings, they made statues for them and worshipped them.

These superstitious beliefs moved from Greece to other regions and eventually to the environment of the Hijaz. However, since the Arabs could not deny the existence of Allah due to the Abrahamic monotheism that remained among them as a legacy, they mixed these beliefs. While believing in the existence of the Almighty God "Allah," they also believed in angels whose relationship with God was that of daughter and father, and they considered stone and wooden idols to be manifestations

[1]: Bulugh al-Arab, Vol. 2, pp. 202-203.

and likenesses of them.

The Quran, in a short but crushing and powerful phrase read in the verses above, says: "These are all contentless names and titles without reality, which you and your ancestors have chosen without any proof or evidence!" Neither can the "god of rain" whom you have named with this title do anything, nor the imaginary gods of the sun, the sea, war, or peace.

Everything is from God, and the entire universe is submissive to His command. The harmony of these different beings with one another is the best proof of the oneness of their Creator; for if there were "gods" (aliha) involved, not only would this harmony not exist, but it would lead to the contradiction and corruption of the world: “Had there been within them [the heavens and earth] gods besides Allah, they both would have been ruined.”

3- The Psychological Source of Idol-Worship

We have learned the historical sources of idol-worship, but from a psychological and intellectual perspective, idol-worship has other sources mentioned in the verses above: following baseless conjectures and the desires of the soul.

It is an illusion and imagination that arises in ignorant individuals, which blind followers take from one another and pass down from generation to generation.

Of course, a deity like an idol—which has no control or supervision over its servants, no Day of Resurrection, no reckoning, no Paradise or Hell, and grants them complete freedom, such that they only turn to it in times of trouble and seek its help in their imagination—is perfectly compatible with rebellious whims and desires, and opens the field for their lusts.

In principle, the desire of the soul (hawa al-nafs) is itself the greatest and most dangerous of idols, the source of the emergence of other idols, and the reason for the thriving market of idol-worship.

4- Again, the Legend of the "Gharanique"!

In the midst of the historical discussion regarding the three idols of the Arabs—al-Lat, al-Uzza, and Manat—it was mentioned that they considered these idols to be "high-soaring birds" (gharanique) from whom they hoped for intercession ("Gharanique" is the plural of "gharnuq"—rhyming with mazdur—meaning a type of white or black water bird). Therefore, they would sometimes follow the mention of these idols' names with the phrase: "Those are the high-soaring birds, and indeed their intercession is hoped for."

Here, a superstitious story is narrated in some books stating that "when the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله reached the verse under discussion (أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ اللَّاتَ وَالْعُزَّى), he personally added these two phrases to it: 'Those are the high-soaring birds, and indeed their intercession is hoped for!' This caused the polytheists to rejoice and consider it a kind of flexibility regarding idol-worship on the part of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله! And at the end of this Surah, when he called the people to prostrate, they also fell into prostration along with the Muslims! This news spread everywhere as the 'conversion of the polytheists to Islam,' and it even reached the ears of the Muslim migrants in Abyssinia. A group became so happy and felt so secure that they returned from their place of migration, Abyssinia, to Mecca." [1]

[1]: This superstitious story is brought in detail by "Tabari" in his History, Vol. 2, pp. 75 onwards.

However, as we explained in detail in the commentary of "Verse 52 of Surah Al-Hajj," this is an unjust attribution and a scandalous lie, the falsehood of which is clarified by many proofs and indications. Those who wove this lie did not consider that the Quran, in the very verses under discussion, explicitly crushes idol-worship and labels it as following raw imagination and the desires of the soul. In the subsequent verses, it also condemns the beliefs of the polytheists with total clarity and intensity, calling it a sign of their lack of faith, knowledge, and awareness, and explicitly commands the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله to separate his account from them and turn away from them.

Given this, how is it possible that those two phrases were from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, or that the polytheists were so afflicted by stupidity that they heard those phrases but ignored the subsequent verses that explicitly demolish idol-worship, and ultimately rejoiced and prostrated with the believers at the end of the Surah?! The truth is that the creators of this legend forged it very clumsily and without study.

It is possible that during the recitation of this verse (أَفَرَأَيْتُمُ اللَّاتَ وَالْعُزَّى...) by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, suddenly Satan or a satanic person from among the gathered polytheists added those two phrases (since these two phrases had become something of a slogan for them which they used to follow the names of the three idols), and a group was temporarily misled.

But neither does their prostration at the end of this Surah make sense, nor does any "flexibility" by the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله regarding idol-worship. The entirety of the Quranic verses and the history of his life testify to the fact that he never showed the slightest flexibility in the matter of fighting idol-worship in any shape or form, and he accepted no proposal in this regard. This is because the whole of Islam was summarized in Monotheism and "La ilaha illa Allah" (There is no god but Allah), and how could the Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله bargain over the core content of Islam?! We have provided more proofs and arguments regarding this under Verse 52 of Surah Al-Hajj (Vol. 14, pp. 141 to 145).

Ayahs

أَمْ لِلْإِنسَـٰنِ مَا تَمَنَّىٰ (٢٤)
Shall man have whatever he yearns for? (24)
فَلِلَّهِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةُ وَٱلْأُولَىٰ (٢٥)
Yet to Allah belong this world and the Hereafter. (25)
۞ وَكَم مِّن مَّلَكٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ لَا تُغْنِى شَفَـٰعَتُهُمْ شَيْـًٔا إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ أَن يَأْذَنَ ٱللَّهُ لِمَن يَشَآءُ وَيَرْضَىٰٓ (٢٦)
How many an angel there is in the heavens whose intercession is of not any avail, except after Allah permits whomever He wishes and approves of! (26)

Commentary

Intercession is Also by His Permission

These verses continue to pursue and condemn the superstition of idol-worship, serving as a continuation of the preceding verses.

First, it addresses the baseless desires of the idolaters and the expectations they had of their idols, saying: "Or shall man have whatever he desires?" (أَمْ لِلْإِنْسانِ ما تَمَنَّى).

Is it possible for these soulless and worthless bodies to rise for his intercession in the presence of Allah? Or to provide him refuge in the face of difficulties and afflictions in this world and the Hereafter?

"While the Hereafter and the first (life) belong only to Allah" (فَلِلَّهِ الْآخِرَةُ وَ الْأُولى‌).

The world of causes revolves around the axis of His will, and whatever any being possesses is by the blessing of His existence. Intercession originates from Him, and the resolution of problems is also in the hand of His power.

It is noteworthy that He mentions the Hereafter first, and then the world, because that which occupies man's thoughts the most is salvation in the Hereafter, and Allah's sovereignty in the next abode is more manifest than in this one.

In this way, the Quran completely causes the polytheists to despair and lose hope in the intercession of idols and the resolution of problems through them, taking away their excuse that: "We worship them only so that they may be our intercessors with Allah":

وَيَقُولُونَ هَؤُلَاءِ شُفَعَاؤُنَا عِنْدَ اللَّهِ (Yunus-18).

In the commentary of the two aforementioned verses, another possibility exists: drawing attention to the existence of the Lord through man's inability to attain his desires and wishes. In the first verse, it asks in the form of a rhetorical negation: "Does man attain all his desires?" Since the answer to this question is certainly negative—as man never achieves many of his desires and, so to speak, takes them to the grave—this indicates that the management of this universe is in the hand of Another, and it is His will that governs this world. Therefore, in the second verse, it says that since this is the case, the Hereafter and this world belong to Allah.

This meaning is similar to what is found in the famous saying of Ali عليه السلام:

عرفت اللَّه سبحانه بفسخ العزائم و حل العقود و نقض الهمم:

"I recognized Allah, the Sublimely Purified, through the breaking of determinations, the undoing of contracts, and the frustration of intentions!" [1] Combining this interpretation with the

[1]: "Nahj al-Balagha," Short Sayings, Saying 250.

previous interpretation is also not unlikely.

In the last verse under discussion, to further emphasize this issue, it adds: "And how many an angel there is in the heavens whose intercession avails nothing except after Allah has given permission to whom He wills and is pleased with" (وَ كَمْ مِنْ مَلَكٍ فِي السَّماواتِ لا تُغْنِي شَفاعَتُهُمْ شَيْئاً إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ أَنْ يَأْذَنَ اللَّهُ لِمَنْ يَشاءُ وَ يَرْضى‌).

When the angels of heaven, with all their greatness, are not capable of intercession even collectively except by the permission and pleasure of the Lord, what do you expect from these mindless idols devoid of any value? Where the wings of sharp-flown eagles fall away, what can weak mosquitoes accomplish? Is it not shameful that you say, "We worship these idols so they may be our intercessors before Allah"?! The expression "Kam" (How many/Many) here implies generality; that is, none of the angels can intercede without His permission and pleasure. This expression in the Arabic language is sometimes used in a collective sense, just as the word "Kathir" (Many) in verse 70 of Surah Al-Isra implies generality: وَ فَضَّلْناهُمْ عَلى‌ كَثِيرٍ مِمَّنْ خَلَقْنا تَفْضِيلًا:

"We have preferred the children of Adam over the generality of Our creations." Likewise, in verse 223 of Surah Ash-Shu'ara regarding the devils, we read: وَ أَكْثَرُهُمْ كاذِبُونَ: "Most of them are liars," while we know that all of them are liars. [1] However, the difference between "Idhn" (Permission) and "Rida" (Pleasure) is that "Idhn" is used when a person manifests his internal satisfaction, but "Rida" is broader than that,

[1]: The plural pronoun in "shafa'atuhum" (their intercession), despite "malak" (angel) being singular, is to respect the concept of the speech which has a collective meaning.

meaning the harmony of nature with the performance of an act or thing. Since someone may sometimes give permission while not having heart-felt satisfaction, the issue of "Rida" is mentioned after "Idhn" in the above verse for emphasis, even though in the case of Allah Almighty, permission is not separate from pleasure, and dissimulation (Taqiyyah) has no meaning regarding Him.

Key Points

1- The Expansion of the Scope of Desires!

Desire and wishing (Tamanni) stem from the limitation of man's power and his inability; for whenever he is fond of something and does not reach it, it takes the form of a wish and desire. If "wanting" were always "attaining," and he immediately reached whatever he wanted, desire would have no meaning.

Of course, man's desires are sometimes truthful and stem from his high spirit, serving as a factor for movement, effort, struggle, and his evolutionary journey—such as when a person desires to be foremost in the world in knowledge, piety, character, and reputation.

But it often happens that these desires are false, and contrary to truthful desires, they are a source of negligence, ignorance, sedation, and backwardness—such as the desire to reach eternal life, immortality on earth, taking control of all wealth and riches, ruling over all humans, and other such delusions.

For this reason, Islamic narrations encourage people to pursue good desires. In a hadith from the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, we read: "Whoever desires something that is pleasing to Allah, the Almighty and Majestic, shall not leave this world until he is given it!" [1] And it is understood from some narrations that if he does not reach it in this world, he will reach its reward and recompense. 2

2- A Word Regarding Intercession

The last verse is among those that clearly inform of the possibility of intercession by angels. Where they have the right of intercession by the permission and pleasure of Allah, the Prophets and the Infallible Awliya (Saints) possess such a right by priority.

However, it must not be forgotten that the above verse explicitly states: this intercession is not unconditional; rather, it is conditional upon the permission and pleasure of Allah. Since His permission and pleasure are not without criteria, there must be a connection between the person and Him so that He grants permission for his intercession to those near His station. It is here that the hope of intercession becomes an educational school for man, preventing the severing of all his ties with Allah. [3]

[1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 71, p. 261 (Chapter on the Reward of Desiring Good Things).

[3]: The expression "for whom He wills" (لِمَنْ يَشاءُ) mentioned in the above verse may refer to the humans for whom Allah permits intercession, or it may refer to the angels to whom He gives permission to intercede; however, the first possibility is more appropriate.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ لَيُسَمُّونَ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ تَسْمِيَةَ ٱلْأُنثَىٰ (٢٧)
Indeed those who do not believe in the Hereafter give female names to the angels. (27)
وَمَا لَهُم بِهِۦ مِنْ عِلْمٍ ۖ إِن يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا ٱلظَّنَّ ۖ وَإِنَّ ٱلظَّنَّ لَا يُغْنِى مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ شَيْـًٔا (٢٨)
They do not have any knowledge of that. They follow nothing but conjectures, and conjecture is no substitute for the truth. (28)
فَأَعْرِضْ عَن مَّن تَوَلَّىٰ عَن ذِكْرِنَا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا (٢٩)
So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. (29)
ذَٰلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُم مِّنَ ٱلْعِلْمِ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَن ضَلَّ عَن سَبِيلِهِۦ وَهُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ (٣٠)
That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge. Indeed your Lord knows best those who stray from His way, and He knows best those who are [rightly] guided. (30)

Commentary

Conjecture Never Leads Anyone to the Truth

These verses continue to pursue the theme of the preceding verses regarding the negation of the polytheists' beliefs.

First, He says: "Indeed, those who do not believe in the Hereafter name the angels with female names" (إِنَّ الَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ لَيُسَمُّونَ الْمَلائِكَةَ تَسْمِيَةَ الْأُنْثى‌).

Indeed, such foul and shameful speech only stems from those who do not believe in the reckoning and recompense of deeds; for if they believed, they would not speak so audaciously—speech for which they have not the slightest evidence. Rather, intellectual proofs demonstrate that neither does Allah have a child, nor are the angels daughters. The expression "naming with female names" (تَسْمِيَةَ الْأُنْثى‌) refers to what was mentioned in the previous verses: that these claims are names without content and titles without reality. In other words, they do not exceed the limits of "naming" and possess no objective reality.

Then, pointing to one of the clear proofs of the invalidity of this naming, He adds: "They have no knowledge or certainty regarding this; rather, they follow baseless conjecture, while conjecture never avails a person against the truth, nor does it lead anyone to the truth" (وَ ما لَهُمْ بِهِ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ يَتَّبِعُونَ إِلَّا الظَّنَّ وَ إِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً).

A committed and believing person never speaks without knowledge and awareness, nor does he attribute something to another without proof. Relying on conjecture and imagination is the work of Satan and Satanic individuals, and accepting superstitions and delusions is a sign of deviation and lack of intellect.

It is clear that the word "Zann" (conjecture/suspicion) has two different meanings: sometimes it refers to baseless assumptions which, according to the expressions of the previous verses, are categorized alongside "whims of the soul, illusions, and superstitions." This is the meaning intended in the verses under discussion.

The other meaning refers to conjectures that are rational and justified, which are often consistent with reality and form the basis of the conduct of rational people in daily life—such as the testimony of witnesses in a court of law, the "opinion of experts," or the "apparent meanings of words" and the like. If such conjectures were removed from human life and reliance was placed solely on absolute certainty, the system of life would completely collapse.

Undoubtedly, this type of "Zann" is not included in these verses, and there are numerous indications within these very verses for this meaning. In other words, the second type is actually a form of "conventional knowledge" rather than mere conjecture. Therefore, the argument of those who use this verse (إِنَّ الظَّنَّ لا يُغْنِي مِنَ الْحَقِّ شَيْئاً) and similar ones to negate the validity of "Zann" entirely is not acceptable.

It is also noteworthy that "Zann" in the terminology of jurists and legal theorists (Usuliyyun) means a "preponderant belief" (a belief where one side of a probability is preferred in a person's view). However, linguistically, it has a broad concept that even includes "illusion" (Wahm) and weak probabilities. The conjecture of the idolaters was of this kind: a superstition would appear in their minds as a weak probability, then their whims would spring into action and beautify it, causing them to forget the other, stronger probability opposing it, until it gradually turned into a firm belief, despite having no foundation.

Then, to clarify that this group is not people of reasoning and logic, and that the love of the world and forgetting the remembrance of Allah has submerged them in the quagmire of these delusions and superstitions, He adds: "Since it is so, turn away from those who have turned away from Our remembrance and desire and seek nothing but the material life of this world," and pay them no heed, for they are not worthy of discourse! (فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَ لَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا).

According to some commentators, "remembrance of Allah" (Dhikr) refers to the Quran. Sometimes it is suggested that it refers to logical and intellectual proofs that lead a person to Allah, and it is also suggested that it is the very remembrance of Allah which is the opposite of negligence (Ghaflah).

However, it appears that this expression has a broad meaning encompassing any form of attention to Allah, whether through the Quran, rational proof, the Sunnah, or the remembrance of the Resurrection.

Furthermore, it can be inferred from the verse that there is a relationship between negligence of Allah's remembrance and the pursuit of material things and the glitz of the world. Notably, there is a mutual influence between the two: negligence of Allah's remembrance drives a person toward world-worship, just as world-worship makes a person negligent of Allah's remembrance. Both are accompanied by following one's whims, and naturally, superstitions that align with those whims become attractive to the person and gradually transform into a belief.

It perhaps needs no mentioning that the command to turn away from this group in no way contradicts the propagation of the Message, which is the primary duty of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. This is because propagation, warning, and giving glad tidings are for cases where there is at least a possibility of influence. Where there is certainty of no effect, resources should not be wasted, and after the completion of the proof (Itmam al-Hujjah), one must turn away.

It is also noteworthy that this instruction is not exclusive to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله; rather, it includes all callers to the path of truth, so that they spend their valuable propagation efforts only in fields where there is hope of effect. As for the arrogant, dark-hearted world-worshippers for whose guidance there is no hope, they should be left to themselves after the proof is completed, so that Allah may judge concerning them.

In the final verse under discussion, to prove the intellectual degradation of this group, He adds: "That is the extent of their knowledge!" (ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ‌).

Yes, the peak of their thoughts has ended here: that they propose the myth of "daughters of God" regarding the angels and struggle in the darkness of illusions and superstitions. This is the ultimate point of their ambition: to consign Allah to oblivion, turn toward the world, and exchange their entire human honor and dignity for a few dirhams and dinars.

And at the end, He says: "Indeed, your Lord knows best those who have strayed from His path, and He knows best those who are guided" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنْ ضَلَّ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ وَ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ اهْتَدى‌).

The phrase "That is the extent of their knowledge" (ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ‌) could refer to superstitions such as idol-worship and considering angels as daughters of God—meaning the ultimate awareness of this group is these very delusions.

Or it could refer to their world-worship and captivity in the clutches of materialism—meaning the ultimate limit of their understanding and perception is that they have contented themselves with sleeping, eating, pleasure-seeking, and the fleeting, transient goods and glitz of the world.

In a well-known supplication narrated from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله regarding the acts of the month of Sha'ban, we read: "O Allah, do not make the world our greatest concern, nor the extent of our knowledge" (و لا تجعل الدنيا اكبر همنا و لا مبلغ علمنا). [^1] The end of the verse points to the reality that Allah knows both the straying and the guided well; He subjects one to His wrath and the other to His grace, and on the Day of Resurrection, He will recompense each according to their deeds.

Key Point: The Capital of World-Worshippers

It is noteworthy that in the above verses, while attributing a kind of "knowledge" to the world-worshippers, He considers them astray. This is proof that from the Quranic perspective, sciences whose ultimate goal is only to reach material ends and which do not pursue a higher goal beyond that are not true knowledge; they are misguidance and straying. Incidentally, all the miseries existing in today's world—all the wars, bloodshed, oppressions, transgressions, corruptions, and pollutions—originate from these misguidance-creating sciences; from those whose extent and limit of knowledge is the life of this world, and whose horizon of vision does not exceed their animalistic needs! Indeed, until science becomes a tool for higher goals, it is ignorance; and until it becomes a source of the light of faith and a means in its path, it is misguidance.

[^1]: This supplication, without mentioning its relation to the acts of the month of Sha'ban, is also mentioned in "Majma' al-Bayan" and some other commentaries under the verse in question.

Ayahs

وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ لِيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَسَـٰٓـُٔوا۟ بِمَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَيَجْزِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ بِٱلْحُسْنَى (٣١)
To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth, that He may requite those who do evil for what they have done, and reward those who do good with the best [of rewards]. (31)
ٱلَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَـٰٓئِرَ ٱلْإِثْمِ وَٱلْفَوَٰحِشَ إِلَّا ٱللَّمَمَ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ وَٰسِعُ ٱلْمَغْفِرَةِ ۚ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِذْ أَنشَأَكُم مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِذْ أَنتُمْ أَجِنَّةٌ فِى بُطُونِ أُمَّهَـٰتِكُمْ ۖ فَلَا تُزَكُّوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ ۖ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ ٱتَّقَىٰٓ (٣٢)
Those who avoid major sins and indecencies, apart from [minor and occasional] lapses. Indeed your Lord is expansive in [His] forgiveness. He knows you best since [the time] He produced you from the earth and since you were foetuses in the bellies of your mothers. So do not flaunt your piety: He knows best those who are Godwary. (32)

Commentary

Do Not Praise Yourselves, He Knows You Best!

Since the previous verses spoke of Allah’s knowledge regarding the strayed and the guided, the verses under discussion continue this theme, adding: "To Allah belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (وَلِلَّهِ مَا فِي السَّمَاوَاتِ وَمَا فِي الْأَرْضِ).

Absolute ownership of the universe belongs to Him, as does absolute sovereignty; for this reason, the governance of the universe is also in His hand. Since this is the case, none but Him is worthy of worship or intercession.

His great purpose for this vast creation is to advance humanity—the crown of creation—along the path of perfection through the systems of creation (takwini) and legislation (tashri’i), and the education and training provided by the Prophets.

Thus, at the end of the verse, as a result of this ownership, He says: "That He may recompense those who do evil with what they have done, and recompense those who do good with the best [reward]" (لِيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَسَاءُوا بِمَا عَمِلُوا وَيَجْزِيَ الَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا بِالْحُسْنَى) [1].

He then proceeds to describe this "doer of good" group, saying: "Those who avoid the major sins and immoralities, except for the small lapses" (الَّذِينَ يَجْتَنِبُونَ كَبَائِرَ الْإِثْمِ وَالْفَوَاحِشَ إِلَّا اللَّمَمَ). "Kabā’ir" is the plural of kabīrah (major), and "ithm" (sin) originally refers to an act that keeps a person

[1]: The "Lām" in "li-yajziya" is the "Lām of Purpose" (Lām al-Ghāyah); therefore, recompense and punishment are the purpose and goal of creation. Although some consider it related to "A’lam" (He knows best) in the previous verse and regard the phrase "To Allah belongs..." as a parenthetical sentence, this possibility seems remote.

away from goodness and reward; hence, it is usually applied to sins.

"Lamam" (rhyming with qalam), according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, means approaching a sin. Small sins are also referred to as lamam. This word is originally derived from the root ilmām, which means approaching something without actually doing it, and it is sometimes applied to small or few things (its application to minor sins is for this reason).

Commentators have mentioned interpretations for "lamam" within this scope: some interpret it as "minor sins," some as "the intention to sin without committing it," and some as "insignificant transgressions."

It has also been said that "lamam" includes any kind of sin, whether minor or major, provided it has not become a habit—occurring only occasionally, after which the person remembers [God] and repents.

In Islamic narrations, various interpretations for this word have appeared. In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read regarding the interpretation of this verse: "It is the sin that a man commits, then stays away from it for as long as Allah wills, then commits it again" (meaning it is never his constant habit) [1]. In another hadith from the same Imam عليه السلام, we read: "Lamam is when a man approaches a sin and then seeks Allah’s forgiveness for it" [2]. Other narrations have been reported with the same meaning.

[1] and [2]: Al-Kafi, Volume 2, Book of Faith and Disbelief, Chapter on Al-Lamam (page 320).

The context within the verse also testifies that "lamam" means sins that occasionally occur from a person, who then realizes and abandons them; for the exception of "lamam" from "major sins" (considering that the apparent form of the exception is a "connected exception") is evidence for this meaning.

Furthermore, in the following phrase, the Quran says: "Indeed, your Lord is vast in forgiveness" (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ وَاسِعُ الْمَغْفِرَةِ). This is also proof that a sin has occurred which requires the Lord’s forgiveness, not merely an intention or approaching a sin without committing it.

In any case, the point is that the doers of good may have a slip, but sinning is contrary to their nature and character. Their souls and hearts are always pure, and impurities are merely accidental; therefore, as soon as they commit a sin, they feel regret and ask Allah for forgiveness, as we read in verse 201 of Al-A'raf: "Indeed, those who fear Allah, when an impulse from Satan touches them, they remember [Him] and at once they have insight" (and repent).

A similar meaning appears in verse 135 of Surah Al-Imran, describing the "God-fearing" and "doers of good": "And those who, when they commit an immorality or wrong themselves, remember Allah and seek forgiveness for their sins." All of these are evidence for the interpretation given for "lamam."

We conclude this discussion with another hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, who said in response to a question about the interpretation of the verse: "The doer of lamam is the servant who commits sin after sin, but it is not his saliqah, meaning his nature" [1].

In the continuation of the verse, to emphasize the justice of the Lord in the matter of reward and punishment, it speaks of His infinite knowledge that encompasses all servants and their deeds, saying: "He is most knowing of you from when He produced you from the earth and when you were fetuses in the wombs of your mothers" (هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِكُمْ إِذْ أَنْشَأَكُمْ مِنَ الْأَرْضِ وَإِذْ أَنْتُمْ أَجِنَّةٌ فِي بُطُونِ أُمَّهَاتِكُمْ) [2]. The creation of man from the earth is either in reference to his initial creation through Prophet Adam, who was created from dust, or in reference to the fact that all the constituent materials of a human being are taken from the earth, which through nutrition affects the composition of the sperm and then the stages of fetal development. In any case, the goal is that Allah has been aware of every detail of your existence from the time your components were scattered within the dust of the earth, and from the day you were an insignificant drop in the mother's womb within the dark veils of the uterus. Given this, how could He be unaware of your actions?!

This expression is also a prelude to the following statement: "So do not claim purity for yourselves; He is most knowing of who fears Him" (فَلَا تُزَكُّوا أَنْفُسَكُمْ هُوَ أَعْلَمُ بِمَنِ اتَّقَى). He needs neither your introduction nor the description of your good deeds; He is aware of both your actions and the level of your sincerity, and He even knows you better

[1]: Al-Kafi, Volume 2, Chapter on Lamam, page 321. [2]: "Ajinnah" is the plural of "Janin," meaning the child who is in the mother's womb.

than you know yourselves, being fully aware of internal attributes and external deeds.

Some commentators have said this verse was revealed regarding a group who, after performing prayer and fasting, would praise themselves, saying: "Our prayer was such, and our fasting was so!" The above verse was revealed and forbade them from this act [1].

Key Points

1. The Infinite Knowledge of Allah Once again, these verses refer to the issue of Allah’s knowledge and its infinite scope, but the expression is a new one, as it relies on two points that are among the most hidden and complex states of man: the state of man's creation from dust—at which the minds of scientists are still bewildered, wondering how a living being can emerge from non-living matter. Certainly, such an event occurred in the past, whether regarding humans or other living creatures, but under what conditions is unknown. This issue is so complex and mysterious that its secrets remain hidden from human science to this day.

The other is the issue of the mysterious transformations of human existence during the fetal period, which is also among the most enigmatic aspects of human creation. Although a shadow of it has been discovered by human science, there are still many mysterious issues and unanswered questions regarding the fetus.

How could One who is aware of all the secrets of human existence and its transformations in these two states, and who guides, leads, and nurtures him, not be aware of his actions and deeds, and not give each their necessary recompense? Thus, this infinite knowledge is the backbone of His absolute justice.

2. What are "Major Sins" (Kabā’ir al-Ithm)? Regarding major sins, which are mentioned in several verses of the Quran [1], commentators on one hand, and jurists and hadith scholars on the other, have spoken extensively.

Some consider all sins to be major, because against the Great Allah, every sin is great. While others consider "major" and "minor" to be relative matters, viewing every sin as minor compared to a more significant sin, and major compared to a smaller one. A group has considered the criterion for being major to be the promise of divine punishment for it within the text of the Quran. It has also been said that a "major sin" is any sin for which a legal penalty (hadd) is prescribed.

However, it is best to say that, given that the term "major" indicates the gravity of the sin, any sin that meets one of the following conditions is considered major: A) Sins for which Allah has promised punishment. B) Sins that are mentioned with gravity in the eyes of the religious law and the language of narrations. C) Sins that are counted in religious sources as greater than a sin already classified as major.

[1]: Surah An-Nisa verse 31, Surah Ash-Shura verse 37, and the verses under discussion.

D) And finally, sins that are explicitly stated to be major in authentic narrations.

In Islamic narrations, the number of major sins is mentioned differently. In some, their number is seven (murder, disobedience to parents, usury, returning to the land of disbelief after migration, accusing chaste women of adultery, consuming the property of orphans, and fleeing from Jihad) [1]. In others, the number is seven, with the difference that instead of disobedience to parents, "whatever Allah has made the Fire obligatory for" is mentioned. In others, the number is ten, in some nineteen, and in some, a much higher number is seen [2]. This difference in counting the number of major sins is because not all major sins are equal; rather, some carry more importance, or in other words, are the "greatest of the major sins" (akbar al-kabā’ir). Therefore, there is no contradiction between them.

3. Self-Praise and Self-Purification The ugliness of this act is such that it has become a proverb: "A man's purification of himself is ugly" (tazkiyat al-mar'i nafsahu qabīhah). The primary source of this unpleasant act is a lack of self-knowledge; for if a person knew himself well—his smallness before the greatness of the Lord, the insignificance of his deeds compared to the heavy responsibilities he bears, and the great blessings Allah has bestowed upon him—he would never step onto the path of self-praise. Pride, negligence, superiority complexes, and ignorant thoughts are other motives for this ugly act.

Since self-praise expresses a person's belief in his own perfection, it is a cause of his stagnation, for the secret of perfection is "confession of shortcoming" and accepting the existence of flaws and weaknesses. For this reason, the saints of Allah were always confessing their shortcomings regarding divine duties and forbade people from self-praise and regarding their own deeds as great.

In a hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام regarding the interpretation of the verse under discussion ("So do not claim purity for yourselves"), it is stated: "None of you should boast about the abundance of his prayers, fasting, zakat, and rituals, for Allah, the Almighty and Sublime, knows best who is God-fearing" [1]. The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, in one of the letters he wrote to Mu'awiyah, reminding him of very important matters, says: "Were it not for what Allah has forbidden regarding a man's purification of himself, the speaker would have mentioned many virtues that the hearts of the believers recognize and the ears of the listeners do not reject" (the speaker referring to the Imam عليه السلام himself) [1].

[1]: Wasa'il, Volume 11, "Chapters on the Jihad of the Soul," Chapter 46, Hadith 1. [2]: For further explanation, refer to the aforementioned source (Chapter 46 of the Chapters on the Jihad of the Soul), where 37 hadiths regarding the identification of major sins are mentioned. [1]: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Volume 5, page 165. [1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Letter 28.

A detailed discussion on this subject also appears in Volume 3 under verse 49 of Surah An-Nisa (page 413 onwards).

It should be noted that sometimes necessities require a person to introduce himself with all the merits he possesses, because without it, sacred goals might be trampled. There is a great difference between such speech and self-praise or self-purification. An example of this is the sermon of Imam al-Sajjad عليه السلام in the Mosque of Damascus, when he wanted to introduce himself and his family to the people of Damascus so that the Umayyad conspiracy regarding the martyrs of Karbala being "outsiders" (khawarij) would fail and their satanic plans would be thwarted.

In a narration from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we also read that when he was asked about the issue of praising oneself, he said: "Sometimes it becomes necessary due to certain requirements." He then argued using two instances of the words of the Prophets mentioned in the Quran: first, Joseph, who suggested to the Vizier of Egypt to make him the treasurer of the country, adding: "Indeed, I am a knowing guardian" (إِنِّي حَفِيظٌ عَلِيمٌ); and the other regarding the great Prophet of Allah, Hud, who addressed his people saying: "I am to you a trustworthy adviser" (أَنَا لَكُمْ نَاصِحٌ أَمِينٌ).
