Circumstances of Revelation

Most commentators have narrated a circumstance of revelation for the above verses, but these accounts are not entirely consistent. The### Circumstances of Revelation:

Most commentators have narrated a circumstance of revelation for the above verses, but these accounts are not entirely consistent. The two most well-known among them are as follows:

1- These verses refer to the story of Uthman; he possessed great wealth and used to spend from it in charity. One of his relatives named "Abdullah bin Sa'd" said to him: "If you continue like this, nothing will remain for you." Uthman replied: "I have committed sins and I wish to seek Allah's pleasure and forgiveness through this." Abdullah said: "If you give me your riding camel with its saddle and gear, I will take all your sins upon my own neck!" Uthman did so and took witnesses to this contract, and thereafter withheld his charity. (The above verses were revealed and severely rebuked this act, clarifying the truth that no one can bear the burden of another's sin, and the result of every person's effort and struggle returns only to himself.) [^1]

2- The verse is about "Walid bin Mughirah." He came to the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and drew close to Islam. Some of the polytheists reproached him, saying: "You have abandoned the religion of our elders, deemed them astray, and imagined they are in the fire of Hell!" He said: "Truly, I fear the punishment of Allah!" The reproacher said: "If you give me some of your wealth and return to polytheism, I will take your punishment upon my own neck!" Walid bin Mughirah did this, but he did not pay the wealth he was supposed to give except for a small portion! The above verse was revealed and rebuked Walid for turning away from faith. [^1]

[^1]: This circumstance of revelation was cited by "Tabarsi" in "Majma' al-Bayan," and other commentators like "Zamakhshari" in "Al-Kashshaf" and "Fakhr Razi" in "Tafsir al-Kabir" have also cited it. "Tabarsi" adds after quoting it: This account is narrated from Ibn Abbas, Suddi, Kalbi, and a group of commentators. [^1]: This circumstance of revelation has also been narrated by "Majma' al-Bayan," "Qurtubi," "Ruh al-Bayan," "Ruh al-Ma'ani," and some other commentaries.

Commentary

Everyone is Responsible for Their Own Deeds

In the preceding verses, the discussion was that Allah punishes the evildoers for their bad deeds and rewards the righteous. Since some might imagine that one person can be punished for another's sin, or that one can take another's sin upon themselves, these verses set out to negate this delusion and explain this important Islamic principle: that the result of every person's actions returns only to themselves.

First, He says: "Have you seen him who turned away (from Islam or from charity)?" (أَ فَرَأَيْتَ الَّذِي تَوَلَّىٰ).

"And gave a little and then withheld (from charity or from paying more)?" (thinking that another could bear the burden of his sins). (وَ أَعْطىٰ قَلِيلًا وَ أَكْدىٰ) [^1]

"Does he have knowledge of the Unseen, so that he sees (that others can carry his sins)?" (أَ عِنْدَهُ عِلْمُ الْغَيْبِ فَهُوَ يَرىٰ). Who has come from the Resurrection and brought news to them that individuals can take bribes and take the sins of others upon their necks?! Or who has come from Allah and informed them that Allah is pleased with such a transaction?! Is it anything other than weaving together delusions and getting caught in the web of these fantasies to escape the burden of responsibility?!

[^1]: "Akda" (أكدى) originally comes from "Kudyah" (on the weight of Hujrah), meaning the hardness and solidity of the ground; it was later used for stingy and miserly individuals.

After this severe Quranic objection, it proceeds to state a general principle that existed in other heavenly religions as well, saying: Has he who abandoned charity (or faith) based on these imaginary promises, wanting to free himself from divine punishment by paying a small amount of wealth, "not been informed of what was revealed in the scriptures of Moses?" (أَمْ لَمْ يُنَبَّأْ بِما فِي صُحُفِ مُوسىٰ).

"And also what was revealed in the book of Abraham, the same Abraham who fulfilled his duty completely?" (وَ إِبْراهِيمَ الَّذِي وَفَّىٰ) [^1] That same great Prophet who fulfilled all divine covenants and promises, performed the right of his messengership, and feared no difficulty, threat, or harm in propagating His religion.

[^1]: "Waffa" (وفى) is from the root "Tawfiyah," meaning full bestowal and performance.

The same one who was placed in the crucible of various trials, and even took his son to the place of sacrifice by Allah's command and placed the knife to his throat, emerging proud and triumphant from all these tests, such that He granted him the high station of leading the creation, as we read in Surah Al-Baqarah, verse 124: "And remember when Abraham was tried by his Lord with certain commands, and he fulfilled them. He said: 'Indeed, I will make you a leader for the people.'"

Some commentators, in explaining this verse, have said: "He gave his body to the fires, his heart to the Most Merciful, his son for sacrifice, and his wealth for his brothers." [^1]

"Has he not been informed that in all these heavenly books, this decree was revealed: that no bearer of burdens shall bear the burden of another?" (أَلَّا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرىٰ). "Wizr" (وِزْر) is originally derived from "Wazar" (on the weight of Khatar), which means mountain refuges. Later, the word "Wizr" was applied to heavy loads because of their similarity to massive mountain rocks, and subsequently, it was applied to sin because it places a heavy burden on a person's shoulders. The meaning of "Wazirah" is a human being who bears a burden. [^2]

[^1]: "Ruh al-Bayan," Vol. 9, p. 246. [^2]: The femininity of "Wazirah" is because it is an adjective for "Nafs" (soul/self) which is omitted, and likewise the femininity of "Ukhra."

Then, for further clarification, it adds: "Does he not know that it has come in these heavenly books that for man there is nothing except what he strives for?" (وَ أَنْ لَيْسَ لِلْإِنْسانِ إِلَّا ما سَعىٰ) [^1] "Sa'y" (سعى) originally means walking fast but not reaching the stage of running, but it is mostly used to mean effort and striving, because during effort and striving in tasks, a person performs fast movements, whether the work is good or evil. It is interesting that He does not say: man's benefit is the work he has done, but rather says it is the striving that has resulted from him. This indicates that the important thing is the effort and struggle, even if the person occasionally does not reach his destination or goal; if his intention is good, Allah gives him a good reward, for He is the buyer of intentions and wills, not just completed works!

"And does he not know that his striving will soon be seen?" (وَ أَنَّ سَعْيَهُ سَوْفَ يُرىٰ). Not only the results of this striving and effort, whether in the path of good or evil, but his deeds themselves will be made manifest before him on that Day, just as He says elsewhere: "The Day every soul will find what it has done of good present before it" (Al-Imran: 30). And also regarding the observation of good and evil deeds in the Resurrection, we read in Surah Al-Zalzalah, verses 7 and 8: "So whoever does an atom's weight of good will see it, and whoever does an atom's weight of evil will see it!"

"Then he will be recompensed for it with the fullest recompense" (ثُمَّ يُجْزاهُ الْجَزاءَ الْأَوْفىٰ) [^2] The meaning of "Al-Jaza' al-Awfa" is a recompense that is exactly in proportion to the deed. Of course, this does not contradict divine grace regarding good deeds being rewarded tenfold, or hundreds, or thousands of times over. The view of some commentators who took "Al-Jaza' al-Awfa" to mean only the greater reward for good deeds does not seem correct, because this verse also includes sins; rather, the primary discussion of the verse is about the burden and sin (take note).

[^1]: "Ma" in "Ma Sa'a" is Masdariyah (infinitival). [^2]: The deputy agent (Na'ib Fa'il) of "Yujza" is a pronoun referring to "man," and the pronoun attached to it refers to "deed" with the omission of the preposition. The estimation was: "Then man is recompensed for his deed with the fullest recompense." Zamakhshari says in "Al-Kashshaf": "It is possible the preposition is not estimated, as it is said 'the servant is recompensed his striving'" (but it should be noted that usually "Allah recompensed him for his deed" is said; "Allah recompensed him his deed" is rare). The expression "Al-Jaza' al-Awfa" can be another object for "Yujza" or a cognate accusative (Maf'ul Mutlaq).

Key Points

1- Three Important Islamic Principles

In the above verses, reference is made to three established Islamic principles, which were also recognized as established principles in previous heavenly books:

A- Everyone is responsible for their own sins. B- Everyone's benefit in the Hereafter is only their own striving and effort. C- Allah gives everyone a complete recompense for their deeds.

In this way, the Quran strikes a line of cancellation through many delusions and superstitions held by common people, or those that have occasionally become a belief in some religions. Through this, the Quran not only negates the belief of the Arab polytheists during the Age of Ignorance—who believed one human could take on the sins of another—but also draws a red pen over a famous belief that was and is prevalent among Christians, who say: Allah sent His son Messiah to the world to be crucified, suffer torture, and bear the burden of the sins of sinners! It also condemns the wicked actions of a group of priests who, in the Middle Ages, sold letters of indulgence and papers for entitlement to Paradise, and even today continue the issue of "forgiving sins." The logic of reason also necessitates this: that "everyone be responsible for their own deeds and benefit from their own deeds." This Islamic belief causes a person, instead of taking refuge in superstitions or throwing their sin onto the neck of this or that person, to seek effort, struggle, and striving in good deeds, to avoid sin, and whenever a slip occurs or a mistake touches them, to return, repent, and compensate. The educational impact of this belief on humans is completely clear and undeniable, just as the destructive effect of those ignorant beliefs is hidden from no one.

It is true that these verses refer to striving and effort for the Hereafter and seeing its reward in the other world, but its main criterion and standard also encompass this world; meaning that people of faith should not sit waiting for others to work for them and solve their societal problems. Rather, they should gird their loins with determination and rise to effort, struggle, and striving. From these verses, a legal principle in criminal matters is also derived: that punishments always afflict the actual sinners, and no one can take the punishment of another upon themselves.

2- Misuse of the Verse's Content

As we said, these verses, by the context of the preceding and following verses, refer to human efforts for matters of the Hereafter. However, because it is based on a certain rational decree, its result can be generalized, and worldly efforts can also be included, as well as worldly rewards and punishments. But this does not mean that some who have been influenced by socialist schools of thought should cite it to say the concept of the verse is that ownership is obtained only through labor, thereby striking a line of cancellation through the laws of inheritance, Mudharabah (profit-sharing), leasing, and the like. The strange thing is that they speak of Islam and argue with Quranic verses while the issue of inheritance is among the definitive principles of Islam, as are Zakat and Khums—even though the heir has performed no effort or striving for the wealth of the deceased, nor have the recipients of Khums and Zakat, nor in cases of bequests, vows, and the like—while all these matters are mentioned in the Holy Quran. In other words, this is a principle, but usually, there is an exception to every principle. For example, a "son" inheriting from a "father" is a principle, but whenever the son is the murderer of the father or leaves Islam, he will be barred from inheritance. Likewise, the result of everyone's effort reaching them is a principle, but there is no obstacle to transferring it—according to a lease contract which is one of the Quranic principles [^1]—in exchange for something agreed upon by both parties, or transferring it to another through a bequest or vow, which is also in the Quran.

[^1]: This principle appears in the story of Moses and Shu'ayb in Surah Al-Qasas, verse 27.

3- Answering Several Questions

Several questions are raised here that must be answered: First, if everyone's benefit in the Resurrection is only the result of their striving, then what is the meaning of "Intercession" (Shafa'ah)? Another is that in Surah At-Tur, verse 21, regarding the people of Paradise, we read: "We will join their offspring with them," while the "offspring" did not strive in this path. Furthermore, it is stated in Islamic narrations that whenever someone performs good deeds, the result reaches their children.

The answer to all these questions is one sentence: the Quran says man has no right to more than his own striving, but this does not prevent blessings from being given to worthy individuals through the grace and favor of the Lord. "Entitlement" (Istihqaq) is one matter, and "Grace" (Tafaddul) is another, just as He rewards good deeds tenfold and sometimes hundreds or thousands of times over. Moreover, "Intercession"—as we have said in its proper place—is not without account; it also requires a type of effort and striving, and the creation of a spiritual connection with the intercessor. Likewise, regarding the joining of the offspring of the people of Paradise to them, the Quran says in that same verse: "And their offspring followed them in faith"—meaning this is the case if their children follow them in faith.

4- The Scriptures of Abraham and Moses

"Suhuf" (صحف) is the plural of "Sahifah" (صحيفة), which originally means anything spread out, and thus they say "Sahifat al-Wajh" (the surface of the face); later it was also applied to the pages of a book. The meaning of "Suhuf of Moses" in the above verses is the Torah itself, and "Suhuf of Abraham" refers to his heavenly book. The late "Tabarsi" in "Majma' al-Bayan," in the commentary of Surah Al-A'la, narrated a Hadith from the Noble Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, the summary of which is as follows: "Abu Dharr asks: How many were the Prophets of Allah? He says: One hundred and twenty-four thousand! He asks again: How many were their Messengers (Rasuls)? He says: Three hundred and thirteen, and the rest were Prophets (Nabi). (A Messenger is one commissioned with delivery and warning, while the concept of Prophet is more general). He asks again: Was Adam a Prophet? He said: Yes, Allah spoke with him and created him with His hand of power. He asks: How many books has Allah revealed? He says: One hundred and four books: ten scrolls (Sahifah) to Adam, fifty scrolls to Seth, thirty scrolls to Idris, ten scrolls to Abraham (making a total of one hundred scrolls), and the Torah, the Gospel, the Psalms, and the Quran." [^1]

[^1]: "Majma' al-Bayan," Vol. 10, p. 476—this Hadith was also cited by "Ruh al-Bayan" in Vol. 9, p. 246.

5- The Principle of Responsibility for Deeds in Previous Books

It is noteworthy that in the current Torah, in the Book of Ezekiel, the content of some of the verses under discussion also appears, as we read: "The soul that sins shall die. The son shall not bear the guilt of the father, nor the father bear the guilt of the son." [^1] This same meaning specifically regarding murder appears in the Book of Deuteronomy of the "Torah": "Fathers shall not be put to death for their children, nor shall children be put to death for their fathers; a person shall be put to death for his own sin." [^2] Of course, the books of the previous Prophets are not fully in our hands today; otherwise, we would have found more instances regarding this principle.

[^1]: Book of Ezekiel, Chapter 18, verse 20. [^2]: "Torah," Book of Deuteronomy, Chapter 24, verse 16.

Commentary

All Paths Lead to Him!

These verses are a manifestation of Allah's attributes, clarifying both the issue of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the Resurrection (Ma'ad).

In these verses, continuing the previous discussions regarding the recompense of deeds, He says:

"Has he not been informed of what is in the scriptures of Moses and Abraham... And that to your Lord is the finality" (وَ أَنَّ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ الْمُنْتَهى‌).

Not only are the accounting, reward, and punishment in the Hereafter in the hand of His power, but in this world as well, the chain of causes and effects ends at His Holy Essence. All the management of this world originates from His governance, and ultimately, the support of the universe and its beginning and end is the Holy Essence of Allah.

In some narrations regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

"When speech reaches [the essence of] Allah, then withhold (keep silent)!" ^1

This means do not speak about His Essence, for intellects are bewildered there and reach nowhere; contemplating the Infinite Essence is impossible for finite minds, because whatever fits within thought is limited, and it is impossible for Allah to be limited.

Of course, this interpretation expresses another concept for this verse which does not conflict with what we have stated, and both can be combined in the meaning of the verse.

Then, to clarify His sovereignty in the matter of Lordship (Rububiyyah) and the fact that all affairs of this world lead to His Holy Essence, He adds: "And that it is He who makes [one] laugh and weep" (وَ أَنَّهُ هُوَ أَضْحَكَ وَ أَبْكى‌). ^1

"And that it is He who causes death and gives life" (وَ أَنَّهُ هُوَ أَماتَ وَ أَحْيا).

"And that He creates the two mates, the male and the female" (وَ أَنَّهُ خَلَقَ الزَّوْجَيْنِ الذَّكَرَ وَ الْأُنْثى‌).

"From a sperm-drop when it is emitted [into the womb]" (مِنْ نُطْفَةٍ إِذا تُمْنى‌).

These few verses are, in fact, a comprehensive statement and a beautiful explanation of the issue that all matters end with the Lordship and governance of the Lord. For He says: your death and life are in His hand; the continuity of generations through the creation of pairs is also by His design; likewise, all events that occur throughout a human's life are from Him—He makes one weep or laugh, causes death or gives life. In this way, the thread of life from beginning to end all leads to His Holy Essence.

In a hadith, the concept of laughter and crying in this verse is expanded, and its interpretation says: "He makes the sky weep with rain, and makes the earth laugh with plants!" ^1 Some poets have captured this same theme in their poetry: "The spring season is a beautiful season / For the earth laughs from the weeping of the sky!" It is noteworthy that among all human actions, the issue of laughter and crying is emphasized, because these two attributes are specific to humans and either do not exist in other living beings or are very rare.

The nature of the physiological reactions and changes that occur in the human body during laughter and crying, and their connection with spiritual changes, is very complex and amazing. Overall, it can be a clear sign among the signs of the Truth's governance, in addition to the harmony these two have with the issue of life and death.

In any case, the fact that all matters end with Allah's governance and Lordship does not conflict with the principle of human choice and free will, because choice and freedom are also from Him and lead back to Him.

After mentioning matters related to the Lordship and governance of the Lord, He turns to the issue of the Resurrection and says: "Has man not been informed that it was in the previous books that upon Allah is the bringing forth of the other creation?" (وَ أَنَّ عَلَيْهِ النَّشْأَةَ الْأُخْرى‌). "Nash'ah" means the creation and nurturing of something; "the other creation" (al-Nash'at al-Ukhra) is nothing other than the Resurrection.

The expression "upon Him" (it is incumbent upon Allah) is from the perspective that when the Clement Lord created humans, placed responsibilities upon them, and gave them all freedom—and among them there were the obedient and the disobedient, the oppressors and the oppressed, and none reached their final reward or punishment in this world—His wisdom necessitates that there be "another creation" so that justice may be realized.

Furthermore, a Wise Being does not create this vast world for a few days of life with all its hardships; there must certainly be a prelude to an expansive life that is worth this vast program. Or to put it another way, if there were no other creation, the creation of this world would not reach its ultimate goal.

It is also noteworthy that Allah has given such a promise as a certain promise to His servants, and the truthfulness of His word necessitates that His promises be unfailing.

Then He adds: "And that it is He who enriches and gives possession" (وَ أَنَّهُ هُوَ أَغْنى‌ وَ أَقْنى‌). Allah has not only removed human needs in material aspects through His universal grace and provided them with lasting capital, but in spiritual life as well, He has met the needs of humans in the matter of education, training, and perfection through sending messengers, revealing heavenly books, and granting spiritual blessings. "Aghna" is from the root "ghina" meaning self-sufficiency, and "aqna" is from the root "qunyah" meaning the wealth and capital that a person stores. ^1 Therefore, "aghna" means removing current needs, and "aqna" means granting stored blessings, which in material matters are like gardens and properties, and in spiritual matters are like the pleasure and satisfaction of Allah, which is considered the greatest eternal capital.

There is another interpretation here that places "aqna" as the opposite of "aghna," meaning that wealth and poverty are in the hand of His power. Similar to what appears in Surah Ar-Ra'd, verse 26: "Allah extends provision for whom He wills and restricts it." However, this interpretation is not consistent with what is found in linguistic sources, and the above verse cannot be evidence for this meaning.

Finally, in the last verse under discussion, He says: "Does man not know that it was in the previous books that He is the Lord of the star Sirius?" (وَ أَنَّهُ هُوَ رَبُّ الشِّعْرى‌). The emphasis on the "star Sirius" (ash-Shi'ra) specifically—besides the fact that this star is the brightest star in the sky, usually appearing at dawn near the constellation Orion and being completely eye-catching—is because a group of Arab polytheists used to worship it. The Quran says: Why do you worship Sirius? Worship its Creator and Lord.

Additionally, it should be noted that there are two stars in the sky named "Shi'ra"; one appears in the "south," and for this reason, it is called "Sirius of Yemen" (because Yemen is to the south of the Arabian Peninsula), and the other is "Sirius of Damascus" which is located to the north, but the famous one is the "Sirius of Yemen."

Regarding the interesting characteristics of this star, there are other discussions that will come in the Key Points.

Key Points

1- All These Echoes Are From Him!

The discussions in these verses are, in fact, an allusion to the meaning that every kind of governance in this world returns to the Holy Essence of Allah. From the issue of life and death to the complex creation of man from an insignificant sperm-drop, as well as the various events that occur throughout human life—making one weep or laugh in some way—all are from Him.

In the sky, the brightest stars are under His command and Lordship, and on earth, the wealth and self-sufficiency of humans return to His Holy Essence. Naturally, the "other creation" of the Hereafter is also by His command, for that too is a new life in continuation of the life of this world.

This statement, on one hand, defines the line of Monotheism (Tawhid), and on the other hand, the line of Resurrection (Ma'ad), because the Creator of man from an insignificant sperm-drop in the womb is also capable of renewing his life. In other words, these are all expressions of Allah's "Monotheism of Actions" (Tawhid al-Af'ali) and "Monotheism of Lordship" (Tawhid al-Rububiyyah). Indeed, "all these echoes are from Him."

2- The Wonders of the Star Sirius

This star, as we mentioned, is the brightest star in the sky and is known as "Sirius of Yemen" because it is located to the south, and since Yemen was in the south of the peninsula, they called it by this name. A group of Arabs, such as the tribe of Khuza'ah, used to sanctify and worship it, believing it to be the source of certain beings on earth. The Quran's emphasis on the fact that Allah is the Lord of Sirius is to awaken this group and those like them who have mistaken the creature for the Creator and placed the "nurtured" (marbub) in place of the "Nurturer" (Rabb).

This extraordinarily created star, which was called the king of stars due to its extreme brightness, possesses wonders, some of which are mentioned below. Considering that these facts about Sirius were unknown at that time, the Quran's emphasis on this subject is very meaningful.

A: According to research conducted in famous observatories of the world, the immense heat prevailing on the surface of Sirius is estimated to be up to 120,000 degrees Celsius! While the surface temperature of our sun is considered to be only 6,500 degrees, this shows the massive difference in the heat of Sirius compared to the sun.

B: The specific gravity of this star is about 50,000 times heavier than water; meaning the weight of one liter of water there would be equivalent to 50 tons on Earth! Meanwhile, among the planets of our solar system, Mercury, which is the densest, has a specific gravity no more than 6 times that of water. With this description, one must see what element this amazing star is composed of that it is so compressed.

C: The star Sirius appears in our century during the winter season, but in the era of ancient Egyptian astronomers, the appearance of this star coincided with the beginning of summer. It is a massive sphere whose volume is 20 times that of the sun, and its distance from us, compared to the distance of the sun from the earth, is extremely great—so much so that this distance is estimated to be one million times the distance of the sun! We know the speed of light is 300,000 kilometers per second, and sunlight reaches us in 8 minutes and 13 seconds, while its distance is 150 million kilometers. But—if you are not surprised—the first ray from the sphere of Sirius reaches us after about 10 years! Now calculate how great its distance is.

D: Sirius of Yemen has a companion star which is among the mysterious stars of the sky. A scientist named Bessel first realized its existence in 1844, but it was observed with a telescope in 1862. The orbital period of the companion star around the main star is 50 years! ^1 All of this shows how meaningful the Quranic expressions are, and that within its smallest expressions lie hidden truths that, if not fully clear on the day of its revelation, have become clear with the passage of time.

3- A Meaningful Hadith from the Prophet (ص)

In a hadith, it is mentioned that the Holy Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله passed by a group who were laughing. He said: "If you knew what I know, you would weep much and laugh little!" When the Prophet passed from there, Gabriel descended to him and said: "Indeed, it is Allah who makes [one] laugh and weep." The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله returned to them and said: "I had not gone more than forty steps when Gabriel came to me and said: 'Return to them and tell them: Indeed, Allah makes [one] laugh and weep.'" ^1 This indicates that it is not necessary for a believer to be always weeping; both weeping out of fear of Allah and dread of sins is necessary in its place, and laughter at times of joy is also necessary, for all are from Allah.

In any case, these expressions do not conflict with the principle of human choice and free will, because the goal is to state the "Cause of causes" and the Creator of these instincts and emotions. And if elsewhere (in Surah At-Tawbah, verse 82) He said: "So let them laugh a little and weep much as recompense for what they used to earn," it refers to the hypocrites, as the verses before and after it testify.

It is interesting to note that at the beginning of the Surah, He swears by the star when it sets (وَ النَّجْمِ إِذا هَوى‌), and here He speaks of the Lord of Sirius. When we place these two verses side by side, it becomes clear why "Sirius" cannot be a deity, for it too has a setting and a vanishing, and is a prisoner to the laws of creation.

^1: Tafsir "Ali ibn Ibrahim" as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 170. ^1: These verbs, although in the past tense, convey the meaning of the present/future tense. ^1: Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 172.

^1: "Encyclopedia of Islam," entry: Shi'ra; "Farhang-nameh," entry: Star; and "Mosahab Persian Encyclopedia," entry: Shi'ra. ^1: Tafsir "Durr al-Manthur," Vol. 6, p. 130.

Ayahs

وَأَنَّهُۥٓ أَهْلَكَ عَادًا ٱلْأُولَىٰ (٥٠)
that it is He who destroyed the former ‘Ad, (50)
وَثَمُودَا۟ فَمَآ أَبْقَىٰ (٥١)
and Thamud, sparing none [of them]; (51)
وَقَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِّن قَبْلُ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ هُمْ أَظْلَمَ وَأَطْغَىٰ (٥٢)
and the people of Noah before that; indeed they were more unjust and rebellious; (52)
وَٱلْمُؤْتَفِكَةَ أَهْوَىٰ (٥٣)
and He overthrew the town that was overturned, (53)
فَغَشَّىٰهَا مَا غَشَّىٰ (٥٤)
covering it with what covered it. (54)
فَبِأَىِّ ءَالَآءِ رَبِّكَ تَتَمَارَىٰ (٥٥)
Then which of the bounties of your Lord will you dispute? (55)

Commentary

Are All These Lessons Not Enough?!

These verses continue the subjects quoted from the previous scriptures—the Scrolls of Abraham and Moses. In the preceding verses, ten points were mentioned within two sections: the first section addressed the responsibility of every individual for their own actions, and the second section spoke of all paths ultimately leading to the Lord. In the verses under discussion, which focus on a single theme, the discourse concerns the punishment and painful destruction of four tyrannical nations of the past. This serves as a warning to those who defy the aforementioned instructions and lack faith in the Origin and the Resurrection. [^1] First, it asks: Has man not been informed of what was in the former scriptures, "And that He destroyed the first 'Ad?" (وَ أَنَّهُ أَهْلَكَ عاداً الْأُولى‌).

The description of the people of 'Ad as "the first" (al-Ula) is either due to the antiquity of this nation—to the extent that it is customary among Arabs to call anything ancient " 'Adi"—or because there were two nations named 'Ad in history, and the well-known nation whose prophet was Prophet Hud عليه السلام was the first 'Ad. [^2]

Then it adds: "And also Allah destroyed the people of Thamud due to their rebellion and left none of them remaining" (وَ ثَمُودَ فَما أَبْقى‌).

Then, regarding the people of Noah, it says: "And the people of Noah before them" (وَ قَوْمَ نُوحٍ مِنْ قَبْلُ‌).

[^1]: Note that these eleven points all begin with "An" (that). The first of them is in verse 38: "That no bearer of burdens shall bear the burden of another," and the last is in verse 50: "And that He destroyed the first 'Ad."

[^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Tafsir Fakhr Razi.

"For they were even more unjust and more rebellious" (إِنَّهُمْ كانُوا هُمْ أَظْلَمَ وَ أَطْغى‌).

This is because their prophet, Noah, spent a longer duration than all other prophets preaching to them; nevertheless, only a few responded to his call. They exhibited extraordinary persistence and stubbornness in polytheism, idolatry, denial, and the persecution of Noah, the details of which will—God willing—be discussed in the commentary of Surah Nuh.

The people of Lot are the fourth nation mentioned, as it says: "And He overthrew the overturned cities [of the people of Lot]" (وَ الْمُؤْتَفِكَةَ أَهْوى‌). Apparently, a violent earthquake hurled these settlements into the sky, overturned them, and smashed them back to the earth. According to narrations, Gabriel, by God-given power, uprooted them from the earth, turned them upside down, and cast them down.

Then, "He covered them with that which He covered" (فَغَشَّاها ما غَشَّى). [^1] Indeed, a rain of heavenly stones poured down upon them, burying all these overturned cities under a heap of stones. It is true that in the phrasing of this verse and the preceding one, the name of the people of Lot is not explicitly mentioned. However, commentators generally—both here and in verse 70 of Surah At-Tawbah and verse 9 of Surah Al-Haqqah, where the term "al-Mu'tafikat" (the overturned cities) is used—have understood it to mean them. Although some have suggested it encompasses all cities that were afflicted and overturned, other verses of the Quran confirm what the majority of commentators have understood here.

In verse 82 of Surah Hud, it states: "So when Our command came, We turned it upside down and rained upon them stones of layered baked clay" (فَلَمَّا جاءَ أَمْرُنا جَعَلْنا عالِيَها سافِلَها وَ أَمْطَرْنا عَلَيْها حِجارَةً مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ مَنْضُودٍ).

In the commentary of Ali ibn Ibrahim, it is mentioned that "al-Mu'tafikah" (the overturned city) refers to the city of Basra, for we read in a narration that the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, addressed them saying:

"O people of Basra! O people of the overturned land! O army of the woman and followers of the beast!" (Referring to the Battle of the Camel, where Aisha was the leader and the people of Basra followed her camel). [^1] However, it is clear that this expression in the words of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, is a form of application (tatbiq) rather than literal exegesis (tafsir). Perhaps at that time, the people of that city shared similarities in character or divine punishment with the people of Lot.

At the conclusion of this discussion, referring to the collection of blessings mentioned in the preceding verses, it asks in the form of a rhetorical question: "Then which of the favors of your Lord do you doubt?" (فَبِأَيِّ آلاءِ رَبِّكَ تَتَمارى‌). Do you doubt the blessing of life, the origin of creation, or the blessing that Allah does not punish anyone for the crime of another—in short, all that was mentioned on the previous page and emphasized in the Quran?

[^1]: "Ma" in "Ma ghashsha" could be the object or the subject, similar to "By the sky and He who built it." However, the first possibility is more consistent with the literal appearance of the verse. In the first case, the meaning is as we stated above. In the second case, the meaning would be: "The Lord who sent a veil of punishment upon them covered them." In any case, this expression is used to convey the greatness and intensity of something.

[^1]: Tafsir al-Safi, under the verses in question.

Do you doubt the blessing that Allah has kept you safe from the punishments of previous nations and has included you in His forgiveness and mercy? Or the blessing of the revelation of the Quran and the matter of the Message, faith, and guidance? It is true that the addressee in this verse is the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, but its concept includes everyone; rather, the primary targets are other individuals.

"Tatamara" is derived from "Tamari," meaning "disputation accompanied by doubt." [^1] "Ala'" is the plural of "Ala" or "Ila," meaning blessing. Although some of the matters mentioned in the previous verses—including the punishment and destruction of other nations—are not instances of blessing in themselves, they are a great blessing in the sense that they serve as a lesson for others, and also because Allah has protected the Muslims and even the pagans of the Prophet's صلى الله عليه وآله era from such fates.

[^1]: Although the "Tafa'ul" form is usually used when an action is performed by two people toward each other, here it is mentioned as the action of one person, either for emphasis or because of the multiplicity of the instances of blessings (reflect on this).

Ayahs

هَـٰذَا نَذِيرٌ مِّنَ ٱلنُّذُرِ ٱلْأُولَىٰٓ (٥٦)
This is a warner, [in the tradition] of the warners of old. (56)
أَزِفَتِ ٱلْـَٔازِفَةُ (٥٧)
The Imminent [Hour] is near at hand. (57)
لَيْسَ لَهَا مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ كَاشِفَةٌ (٥٨)
There is none who may unveil it besides Allah. (58)
أَفَمِنْ هَـٰذَا ٱلْحَدِيثِ تَعْجَبُونَ (٥٩)
Will you then wonder at this discourse, (59)
وَتَضْحَكُونَ وَلَا تَبْكُونَ (٦٠)
and laugh and not weep, (60)
وَأَنتُمْ سَـٰمِدُونَ (٦١)
while you remain heedless?! (61)
فَٱسْجُدُوا۟ لِلَّهِ وَٱعْبُدُوا۟ ۩ (٦٢)
So prostrate yourselves to Allah and worship Him! (62)

Commentary

Prostrate All Before Him

Following the previous verses which spoke of the destruction of past nations due to their oppression and rebellion, the verses under discussion address the polytheists, disbelievers, and those who deny the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله) call, stating: "This Prophet (or this Quran) is a warner like the warners of old" (هذا نَذِيرٌ مِنَ النُّذُرِ الْأُولى‌).

The statement that the Prophet (or the Quran) is of the same kind as the earlier warners implies that the mission of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) and His divine book, the Quran, is not an unprecedented phenomenon; many similar instances have occurred in the past. Why, then, does it cause you wonder?

Some commentators have also suggested that "this" (هذا) refers to the accounts mentioned in the preceding verses regarding the history of past nations, as these accounts are themselves warners. However, the first two interpretations seem more appropriate.

Then, to make the polytheists and disbelievers more attentive to the danger ahead, He adds: "That which is to draw near has drawn near" (أَزِفَتِ الْآزِفَةُ). Yes, the Resurrection is near; prepare yourselves for questioning, accounting, and recompense.

The use of the term "Azifah" for the Day of Resurrection is due to its proximity and the shortness of time, as this word is derived from "azaf" (rhyming with Najaf), meaning "shortness of time," and naturally encompasses the concept of drawing near.

Naming the Resurrection by this title appears in Verse 18 of Surah Ghafir in addition to the verse under discussion. it is an expressive and awakening term. We read the same concept in another form in Verse 1 of Surah Al-Qamar: "The Hour has drawn near" (اقْتَرَبَتِ السَّاعَةُ). In any case, the proximity of the Resurrection is understandable considering the brevity of the world's lifespan, especially since for everyone who dies, their "minor resurrection" begins.

He then adds: The important point is that "none besides Allah can avert it [on that day], or remove its hardships" (لَيْسَ لَها مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ كاشِفَةٌ) [^1]. "Kashifah" here means the one who removes hardships.

However, some commentators have interpreted "Kashifah" as a factor that delays the Resurrection, and some have taken it to mean "the one who reveals the date of the Resurrection's occurrence," but the first meaning is the most fitting of all.

In any case, the Ruler, the Owner, and the Possessor of power on that day (and always) is Allah. If you seek salvation, reach out to His grace, and if you seek tranquility, place yourselves under the shadow of faith in Him.

In the next verse, He adds: "Do you then wonder at this recital?" (أَ فَمِنْ هذَا الْحَدِيثِ تَعْجَبُونَ‌). This sentence may refer to the issue of the Resurrection mentioned in the previous verses, or to the Quran (as it is referred to as "Hadith" in other verses) [^2], or to the accounts given about the destruction of past nations, or all of these.

Then He says: "And do you laugh and not weep?" (وَ تَضْحَكُونَ وَ لا تَبْكُونَ).

[^1]: The pronoun "laha" refers back to "Azifah," and the femininity of "Kashifah" is because it is an attribute for a hidden "nafs" (soul/entity). Some have also suggested that the "ta" in "Kashifah" is for emphasis (mubalaghah), like "Allamah."

[^2]: Surah At-Tur, Verse 34.

"While you remain in heedlessness, vanity, and sinful amusement?!" (وَ أَنْتُمْ سامِدُونَ‌).

Whereas this is neither the place for laughter nor for heedlessness and ignorance; it is the place for weeping over lost opportunities, abandoned acts of obedience, and the transgressions and sins you have committed. It is the place for awakening and rectifying what has passed, and ultimately, the place for repentance, turning back, and returning to the shadow of Allah's grace.

"Samidun" is derived from "samud" (rhyming with jumud), meaning amusement, vanity, and raising one's head out of pride and arrogance. Originally, it was said of a camel when it walks and raises its head toward the sky out of indifference.

These arrogant, prideful people are occupied with sleep and food like animals, drowned in luxury and pleasure, and are unaware of the painful events and severe punishments that lie ahead and are about to seize them.

In the final verse of this Surah, following the extensive discussions presented regarding the proof of Monotheism (Tawhid) and the negation of polytheism (Shirk), He says: "Now that it is so, prostrate to Allah and worship Him" (فَاسْجُدُوا لِلَّهِ وَ اعْبُدُوا). If you wish to walk upon the straight path of truth, prostrate only to Him, to whose Pure Essence all lines of existence lead. And if you wish not to meet the painful fate of past nations who were caught in the grip of divine punishment due to polytheism, disbelief, and oppression, then worship Him alone.

It is noteworthy that many narrations state that when the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) reached this verse while reciting this Surah, all the believers and disbelievers who heard it fell into prostration. According to one narration, the only person who did not prostrate was Walid bin Mughirah, who (perhaps because he could not bend down to prostrate) took a handful of soil, placed it to his forehead, and prostrated in this manner!

This is not surprising—that even the idolaters fell into prostration—because the captivating tone of this Surah on one hand, its exciting content on the other, the terrifying threats against the polytheists on a third, and the emergence of these blessed verses from the mouth of the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) at the initial stage of the revelation's descent on a fourth, were so captivating, effective, and influential that they involuntarily affected every heart. They pushed aside the veils of obstinacy, stubbornness, prejudice, and selfishness, even if temporarily, and allowed the light of Monotheism to shine into the hearts.

If we ourselves recite this Surah with care and reflection, with presence of heart and attention, and imagine ourselves before the Noble Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) in the atmosphere of the verses' revelation, we will see that—regardless of specific Islamic beliefs—we have no choice but to fall into prostration and bow our heads in submission before the Truth when we reach the final verse.

This is not the first time the Quran has affected the hearts of deniers and attracted them involuntarily. As mentioned in the story of Walid bin Mughirah, when he heard the verses of Surah Fussilat, and the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) recited this verse: "But if they turn away, then say: I have warned you of a thunderbolt like the thunderbolt of 'Ad and Thamud," he stood up, trembled, and his hair stood on end. He returned home in such a state that the polytheists thought he had been completely captivated by the religion of Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله).

Therefore, there is no need to say that because some devils among the jinn or devilish humans, during the recitation of "Have you then considered Al-Lat and Al-'Uzza..." (which speaks of the famous Arab idols), began to describe these idols and said the phrase "Those are the high-flying cranes (Gharaniq)," and for this reason attracted the attention of the polytheists, they prostrated for that reason.

For as we have previously stated in the commentary of these verses, the verses following the mention of these idols severely condemn them and leave no room for doubt, error, or mistake for anyone (for further explanation, refer to the commentary of Verses 19 and 20 of this Surah).

It is necessary to remind that "the above verse" is among the verses where prostration is obligatory upon everyone when recited. The tone of the verse, which uses the imperative form (command), and a command is evidence of obligation, also attests to this meaning. Thus, after Surah "Alif Lam Mim Sajdah" and "Ha Mim Sajdah," this is the third Surah containing an "obligatory prostration" (Sajdah Wajib), although according to some narrations, in terms of the history of revelation, this was the first Surah in which an obligatory prostration verse was revealed.

O Allah! Always shine the lights of Your gnosis into our hearts, so that we worship none but You and prostrate before none but You. O Lord! The key to all goodness is in the hand of Your power; grant us a share of Your best blessings and gifts, namely Your pleasure and satisfaction.

O Lord! Grant us an eye that takes heed, so that we may learn lessons from the fate of the oppressive nations of the past and beware of treading their path.

Amin, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah An-Najm and end of Volume 22 of Tafsir Nemouneh. 15 Rabi' al-Awwal 1406.
